## File: `04_source_map_html.md`

# Source Map

Converted from `04_source_map.html` so the pack remains Markdown-only.

```html
<!doctype html><html><head><meta charset='utf-8'><title>Source Map</title><style>body{font-family:Arial, sans-serif;max-width:1000px;margin:40px auto;line-height:1.45} h1{border-bottom:2px solid #222;padding-bottom:8px} h2{margin-top:32px} .source{border:1px solid #ddd;border-radius:10px;padding:14px;margin:12px 0} .meta{color:#555;font-size:0.92em} .priority{font-weight:bold}</style></head><body><h1>Source Map</h1><p>Clickable source library for the 6-month study plan. Bias notes are included so you can read aggressively without being captured by one school of thought.</p><h2>Advertising effectiveness</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/002224299906300103'><strong>How Advertising Works: What Do We Really Know?</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Demetrios Vakratsas; Tim Ambler (1999) - Peer-reviewed review paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Breaks the simplistic ad -&gt; belief -&gt; purchase model; maps cognition, affect, and experience effects.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic review; older but foundational; not platform-funded</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1509/jmkr.48.3.457'><strong>How Well Does Advertising Work? Generalizations from Meta-Analysis of Brand Advertising Elasticities</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Raj Sethuraman; Gerard J. Tellis; Richard A. Briesch (2011) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Gives empirical benchmarks for how much advertising tends to move sales; prevents magical thinking.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic meta-analysis; publication and method heterogeneity still matter</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://personal.eur.nl/stremersch/artikels/Korkames_Stanley_and_Stremersch_IJRM_2025_print.pdf'><strong>Meta-analysis of Advertising Effectiveness: New Insights from Improved Bias Corrections</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Joseph Korkames; T. D. Stanley; Stefan Stremersch (2025/2026) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis / bias correction - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Important antidote to overconfident marketing claims; focuses on how bias inflates measured ad effects.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic; explicitly focused on correcting publication/misspecification/aggregation bias</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0022242920929288'><strong>A Meta-Analysis of When and How Advertising Creativity Works</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Sara Rosengren; Martin Eisend; Scott Koslow; Micael Dahlen (2020) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Teaches when creative advertising changes responses and when it does not.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic; creativity effects depend heavily on measurement and context</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://ipa.co.uk/knowledge/publications-reports/the-long-and-the-short-of-it-balancing-short-and-long-term-marketing-strategies'><strong>The Long and the Short of It: Balancing Short and Long-Term Marketing Strategies</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Les Binet; Peter Field (2013) - Practitioner/industry effectiveness analysis - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Separates short-term activation from long-term brand building; useful for avoiding click-obsession.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Practitioner and award-case database; useful but not neutral academic evidence</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.gc.cuny.edu/sites/default/files/2021-07/Does-Avertising-Work--Winer.pdf'><strong>Does Advertising Work? Evidence from the Past and Some New Evidence</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Russ Winer (2012) - Academic presentation / review - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Readable overview of advertising elasticities and historical evidence.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Lecture deck; interpret as synthesis, not primary evidence</p></div><h2>Attention and ad avoidance</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.ruf.rice.edu/~lane/papers/banner_blindness.pdf'><strong>Banner Blindness: Web Searchers Often Miss “Obvious” Links</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Jan Panero Benway; David M. Lane (1998/2007) - Usability research paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Primary evidence for why ad-like elements get ignored.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Old web context but human attention pattern remains useful</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nngroup.com/articles/banner-blindness-old-and-new-findings/'><strong>Banner Blindness Revisited: Users Dodge Ads on Mobile and Desktop</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen Norman Group (2018) - UX research article - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Modern applied summary of banner blindness and ad-like UI avoidance.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> UX consultancy; credible but commercial</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nngroup.com/articles/banner-blindness-original-eyetracking/'><strong>Banner Blindness: The Original Eyetracking Research</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen Norman Group (2007) - UX research article - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful visual attention framing for ad/landing page design.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> UX consultancy; interpret alongside academic work</p></div><h2>B2B software buying</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://images.g2crowd.com/uploads/attachment/file/1470753/2025-G2-Buyer-Behavior-Report.pdf'><strong>2025 G2 Buyer Behavior Report</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>G2 (2025) - Vendor/industry report - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Current B2B software buyer behavior, shortlist dynamics, AI/review influence.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Review marketplace with commercial incentive; useful current signal</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://go.trustradius.com/rs/827-FOI-687/images/TrustRadius-Bridging-the-Trust-Gap-B2B-Tech-Buying-in-the-Age-of-AI.pdf'><strong>B2B Tech Buying in the Age of AI</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>TrustRadius (2025) - Vendor/industry report - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Highlights transparent pricing, AI-assisted research, fact-checking, and trust gaps.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Review marketplace with commercial incentive; still useful buyer survey data</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-06-25-gartner-sales-survey-finds-61-percent-of-b2b-buyers-prefer-a-rep-free-buying-experience'><strong>Gartner Sales Survey Finds 61% of B2B Buyers Prefer a Rep-Free Buying Experience</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Gartner (2025) - Analyst research press release - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Important for self-serve proof architecture and low-pressure buying paths.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Analyst firm; full methodology may be gated</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2026-03-09-gartner-sales-survey-finds-67-percent-of-b2b-buyers-prefer-a-rep-free-experience'><strong>Gartner Sales Survey Finds 67% of B2B Buyers Prefer a Rep-Free Experience</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Gartner (2026) - Analyst research press release - <span class='priority'>Useful/current</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Current signal that self-directed buying continues strengthening.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Analyst firm; current but press-release level</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://info.wynter.com/hubfs/B2B%20buying%20journey%20study%20by%20Wynter.pdf'><strong>How B2B SaaS Marketing Leaders Buy Software in 2024</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Wynter (2024) - Vendor research report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good for messaging, shortlists, peer/review/site influence in SaaS.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Wynter sells message testing; small sample; still very tactical</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://goconsensus.com/research/2025-b2b-buyer-behavior-report'><strong>2025 Buyer Behavior Report</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Consensus (2025) - Vendor/industry report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good current view of how product experiences support buyer self-education.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Interactive demo vendor; may overstate demo/product-tour value</p></div><h2>Brand strategy</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://marketingscience.info/news-and-insights/grow-brand-grow'><strong>How Brands Grow</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Byron Sharp (2010) - Book / evidence-based marketing - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Mental availability, physical availability, distinctive assets; useful counterweight to narrow performance marketing.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Ehrenberg-Bass school has strong viewpoint; compare with segmentation/positioning literature</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.distinctivebat.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/The-DNA-of-Distinctive-Brands-Distinctive-BAT-Guide-To-DBAs.pdf'><strong>The DNA of Distinctive Brands: Guide to Distinctive Brand Assets</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Distinctive BAT (2024) - Practitioner guide - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Practical way to apply distinctive brand asset thinking to digital products.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Commercial consultancy; useful framework but verify with independent evidence</p></div><h2>Buyer psychology</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.demenzemedicinagenerale.net/images/mens-sana/Captology_Fogg_Behavior_Model.pdf'><strong>A Behavior Model for Persuasive Design</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>B. J. Fogg (2009) - Academic conference paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Motivation + ability + prompt is the simplest usable model for conversion behavior.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Persuasive design lens; can be misused if not ethically constrained</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://behaviordesign.stanford.edu/resources/fogg-behavior-model'><strong>The Fogg Behavior Model</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Stanford Behavior Design Lab (Current) - Official framework page - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good quick reference for the behavior model.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Official model page; simplified</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://richardepetty.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/01/1986-advances-pettycacioppo.pdf'><strong>The Elaboration Likelihood Model of Persuasion</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Richard E. Petty; John T. Cacioppo (1986) - Foundational persuasion theory chapter - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Separates deep argument processing from surface-cue persuasion.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Classic model; not a conversion playbook by itself</p></div><h2>Customer journey</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/growth-marketing-and-sales/our-insights/the-consumer-decision-journey'><strong>The Consumer Decision Journey</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>David Court; Dave Elzinga; Susan Mulder; Ole Jørgen Vetvik (2009) - Consulting/practitioner model - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful replacement for simplistic linear funnels.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Consulting framework; not peer-reviewed</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/growth-marketing-and-sales/our-insights/the-new-consumer-decision-journey'><strong>The New Consumer Decision Journey</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>McKinsey (2015) - Consulting/practitioner model - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Adds digital feedback loops and active evaluation.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Consulting perspective</p></div><h2>Customer research</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://hbr.org/2016/09/know-your-customers-jobs-to-be-done'><strong>Know Your Customers&#x27; Jobs to Be Done</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Clayton M. Christensen; Taddy Hall; Karen Dillon; David S. Duncan (2016) - HBR article / strategy framework - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Teaches that customers “hire” products to make progress in a situation.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Practitioner/consulting style; use with interviews, not slogans</p></div><h2>Digital advertising measurement</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www-2.rotman.utoronto.ca/~agoldfarb/OnlineAdvertising.pdf'><strong>Online Advertising</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Avi Goldfarb; Catherine Tucker (2011/2014) - Academic handbook chapter - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Explains targeting, measurement, privacy, and economic differences between online and offline advertising.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic overview; older but still foundational</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://marketing.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2020/07/Privacy-Regulation-and-Online-Advertising.pdf'><strong>Privacy Regulation and Online Advertising</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Avi Goldfarb; Catherine Tucker (2011) - Peer-reviewed research paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows how privacy rules and targeting interact with ad effectiveness.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic; context predates today’s privacy stack but mechanisms matter</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nber.org/system/files/working_papers/w19520/w19520.pdf'><strong>Measuring the Effects of Advertising: The Digital Frontier</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Randall A. Lewis; Justin M. Rao; David H. Reiley (2013/2015) - NBER/academic chapter - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Clicks are easy to count; causal ad lift is hard. This paper teaches that distinction.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Authors have worked at ad platforms; still one of the strongest measurement papers</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2367103'><strong>The Unfavorable Economics of Measuring the Returns to Advertising</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Randall A. Lewis; Justin M. Rao (2015) - Peer-reviewed field experiment evidence - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows why measured ROI confidence intervals can be huge; prevents dashboard worship.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Strong empirical design; not a “marketing is useless” paper, a measurement precision paper</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.kellogg.northwestern.edu/faculty/gordon_b/files/fb_comparison.pdf'><strong>A Comparison of Approaches to Advertising Measurement: Evidence from Big Field Experiments at Facebook</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Brett R. Gordon; Florian Zettelmeyer; Neha Bhargava; Daniel Chapsky (2018/2019) - Peer-reviewed field experiment comparison - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Contrasts randomized experiments with observational measurement and shows why endogeneity matters.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Facebook experiment data; platform context matters, but methodology lessons are broad</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://marketing.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2017/08/Johnson-Garrett-PAPER-VERSION-2.pdf'><strong>The Online Display Ad Effectiveness Funnel &amp; Carryover: Lessons from 432 Field Experiments</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Garrett A. Johnson; Randall A. Lewis; Elmar I. Nubbemeyer (2017) - Field experiment meta-study - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful for understanding small ad effects, funnel stages, and carryover.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Display-ad platform setting; still valuable scale and causal design</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://faculty.haas.berkeley.edu/stadelis/BNT_ECMA_rev.pdf'><strong>Consumer Heterogeneity and Paid Search Effectiveness: A Large-Scale Field Experiment</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Thomas Blake; Chris Nosko; Steven Tadelis (2015) - Peer-reviewed field experiment - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows paid search can be weak for already-known brands but useful for new/infrequent users.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> eBay context; generalize carefully</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.davidreiley.com/papers/OnlineAdsOfflineSales.pdf'><strong>Online Ads and Offline Sales: Measuring the Effects of Retail Advertising via a Controlled Experiment on Yahoo!</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Randall A. Lewis; David H. Reiley (2014) - Peer-reviewed controlled experiment - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Important reminder that many ad effects may happen without clicks.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Retail context; still useful for click vs non-click impact</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://hanachoi.github.io/research-papers/choi_et_al_review_display_advertising.pdf'><strong>Online Display Advertising Markets: A Literature Review and Future Directions</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Hana Choi; Carl Mela; Santiago R. Balseiro; Adam Leary (2019) - Academic literature review - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Map of the display advertising ecosystem, auctions, targeting, advertisers, publishers, and consumers.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic review; technical</p></div><h2>Ethics and dark patterns</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/behavioural-public-policy/article/sludge-audits/12A7E338984CE8807CC1E078EC4F13A7'><strong>Sludge Audits</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Cass R. Sunstein (2022) - Peer-reviewed public policy paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Convert ethically by removing unjustified friction; do not add sludge to trap people.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Policy perspective; useful ethical lens</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.oecd.org/content/dam/oecd/en/publications/reports/2024/06/fixing-frictions-sludge-audits-around-the-world_1b4bbf1a/5e9bb35c-en.pdf'><strong>Fixing Frictions: Sludge Audits Around the World</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>OECD (2024) - Government/intergovernmental report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good practical approach to auditing harmful friction.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Public-sector lens; not commercial conversion-focused</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/P214800%2BDark%2BPatterns%2BReport%2B9.14.2022%2B-%2BFINAL.pdf'><strong>Bringing Dark Patterns to Light</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>U.S. Federal Trade Commission (2022) - Government/regulator report - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Clear boundaries between persuasion and deception/manipulation.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Regulator lens; emphasizes consumer harm</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/dark-commercial-patterns_44f5e846-en.html'><strong>Dark Commercial Patterns</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>OECD (2022) - Intergovernmental evidence report - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Defines dark patterns, prevalence, harms, and responses.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Policy/regulator lens; not a growth handbook</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/online-choice-architecture-how-digital-design-can-harm-competition-and-consumers'><strong>Online Choice Architecture: How Digital Design Can Harm Competition and Consumers</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>UK Competition and Markets Authority (2022) - Regulator discussion paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Taxonomy of online choice architecture practices: defaults, scarcity, friction, prompts, ranking, etc.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Competition/consumer protection lens</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/6258499fd3bf7f600e76d9de/OCA_Evidence_Review_Paper_14.4.22.pdf'><strong>Evidence Review of Online Choice Architecture and Consumer and Competition Harm</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>UK CMA (2022) - Regulator evidence review - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Large document with academic evidence behind online choice architecture.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Regulator/evidence review; deep but harm-oriented</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://arxiv.org/abs/1907.07032'><strong>Dark Patterns at Scale: Findings from a Crawl of 11K Shopping Websites</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Arunesh Mathur et al. (2019) - Peer-reviewed HCI paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Empirical taxonomy of dark patterns at scale.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Shopping-site focus; taxonomy useful across digital products</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.oaic.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0018/232623/Public-Report-ICPEN-Dark-Patterns-Sweep.pdf'><strong>ICPEN Dark Patterns in Subscription Services Sweep Public Report</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>ICPEN consumer protection agencies (2024) - International regulator sweep report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Very relevant to SaaS subscriptions, cancellation, and recurring billing.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Regulator lens; subscription focus</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://cprc.org.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/CPRC-Duped-by-Design-Final-Report-June-2022.pdf'><strong>Duped by Design</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Consumer Policy Research Centre (2022) - Consumer policy report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good regional dark-pattern context and examples.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Consumer advocacy lens; Australia-relevant</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.bi.team/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Review-of-Online-Choice-Architecture-and-Vulnerability-July-2024-.pdf'><strong>Review of Online Choice Architecture and Vulnerability</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Behavioural Insights Team (2024) - Research review - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Important for understanding vulnerable users and ethical design.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Policy/behavioral science perspective</p></div><h2>Experimentation</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://exp-platform.com/Documents/2013%20controlledExperimentsAtScale.pdf'><strong>Online Controlled Experiments at Large Scale</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Ron Kohavi et al. (2013) - Academic/practitioner paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> A/B testing fundamentals, scale issues, organizational discipline.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Written by experimentation practitioners; highly practical</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/trustworthy-online-controlled-experiments/4EC18E75D4F1D3A576B28E0CBD49F5B9'><strong>Trustworthy Online Controlled Experiments: A Practical Guide to A/B Testing</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Ron Kohavi; Diane Tang; Ya Xu (2020) - Book - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Best practical guide to running experiments without fooling yourself.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Practitioner-led but rigorous; book is not free in full</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://kdd.org/exploration_files/v12-02-8-UR-Kohavi.pdf'><strong>Unexpected Results in Online Controlled Experiments</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Ron Kohavi; Roger Longbotham (2010) - Academic/practitioner paper - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Teaches humility: experiments often produce counterintuitive results.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Practitioner perspective; valuable failure modes</p></div><h2>Market/channel context</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://datareportal.com/reports/digital-2025-sub-section-brand-discovery'><strong>Digital 2025: How People Discover New Brands</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>DataReportal / GWI (2025) - Global digital behavior report - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows search, social, digital ads, word-of-mouth, and multi-source discovery patterns.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Industry report using survey data; good macro directional signal</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://datareportal.com/reports/digital-2025-global-overview-report'><strong>Digital 2025: Global Overview Report</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>DataReportal / We Are Social / Meltwater (2025) - Global digital report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Macro context: digital behavior, search/social usage, online media time.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Industry synthesis; check methodology</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.kantar.com/campaigns/media-reactions'><strong>Media Reactions 2025</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Kantar (2025) - Industry research report - <span class='priority'>Useful/current</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Channel receptivity matters: the same message performs differently in different environments.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Research vendor; commercial but useful for ad receptivity trends</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.edelman.com/sites/g/files/aatuss191/files/2025-06/2025%20Edelman%20Trust%20Barometer%20Special%20Report%20Brand%20Trust%2C%20From%20We%20to%20Me_FINAL_0.pdf'><strong>2025 Edelman Trust Barometer Special Report: Brand Trust, From We to Me</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Edelman (2025) - Industry trust report - <span class='priority'>Useful/current</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful signal on consumer trust, brand expectations, and skepticism.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> PR firm; trust framing can serve client interests</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nielsen.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/2022/02/2021-Nielsen-Trust-In-Advertising-U.S.-sell-sheet.pdf'><strong>Trust in Advertising Study</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen (2021) - Industry research sell sheet - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Quick view of relative trust channels and message types.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Research product sell sheet; limited detail</p></div><h2>Pricing and monetization</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://marketing.wharton.upenn.edu/wp-content/uploads/2024/05/Morwitz-Vicki-Wroe-Alderson-PAPER-2020.pdf'><strong>Consumer Reactions to Drip Pricing</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>S. Santana; S. Dallas; V. G. Morwitz (2020) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows how price presentation changes behavior and perceived fairness.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Specific pricing mechanism; important ethical boundary</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.jstor.org/stable/pdf/3152164.pdf'><strong>Consumers&#x27; Reactions to Partitioned Prices</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Vicki G. Morwitz; Eric A. Greenleaf; Eric J. Johnson (1998) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Explains why separating price components can change recall and demand.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Classic paper; older and often misused by dark-pattern marketers</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S105774081500042X'><strong>A Review of Research on Partitioned Pricing</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Eric A. Greenleaf et al. (2016) - Peer-reviewed literature review - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Context for price framing and price transparency.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Review literature; not SaaS-specific</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022435922000124'><strong>Behavioral Response to Price: Data-Based Insights and Future Research Directions</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Gurumurthy Kalyanaram et al. (2022) - Peer-reviewed review - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good map of price perception, reference price, and response.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Retail-oriented pricing literature</p></div><h2>SaaS/product-led growth</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3945909'><strong>Converting Free Users to Paid Subscribers in the SaaS Context</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Hongshuang (Alice) Li (2022) - Peer-reviewed SaaS marketing paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Directly connects marketing touchpoints, message content, usage, and conversion.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Single SaaS-firm data; excellent relevance, limited generality</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12217587/'><strong>Longer or Shorter? A Large-Scale Randomized Field Experiment on Free Trial Duration in the Freemium Model</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Ling Zhang; Jiang Duan (2025) - Open-access field experiment - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Trial length affects adoption and delayed conversion differently from immediate conversion.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> One SaaS company; still valuable because randomized and large-scale</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/155324/1/880313099.pdf'><strong>Freemium Pricing: Evidence from a Large-Scale Field Experiment</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Julian Runge et al. (2016) - Working paper / field experiment - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Helps think beyond “free = growth” toward long-term profit and conversion.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Game/software context; use as freemium mechanics evidence</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/mar.21211'><strong>The Zero-Price Effect in Freemium Business Models</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Thomas Niemand et al. (2019) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Explains why free tiers can both reduce friction and anchor users against paying.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Behavioral experiments; not a complete GTM model</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1567422322000370'><strong>Designing Freemium with Usage Limitation</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Takumi Kato (2022) - Peer-reviewed modeling paper - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful for deciding whether to limit features, seats, usage, storage, or outputs.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Model-based; validate against real product data</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://chartmogul.com/reports/saas-conversion-report-2/'><strong>The SaaS Conversion Report</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>ChartMogul (Current) - Vendor/industry benchmark - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Benchmarks for trial/freemium conversion; useful but not law.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Subscription analytics vendor; benchmarks are directional</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://openviewpartners.com/benchmarks/'><strong>Product Benchmarks / Product-Led Growth Benchmarks</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>OpenView (Current) - VC/practitioner benchmark - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good for SaaS benchmarks, PLG vocabulary, and metrics literacy.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> VC lens; data set selection and incentives matter</p></div><h2>Skepticism and trust</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://academic.oup.com/jcr/article/21/1/1/1853712'><strong>The Persuasion Knowledge Model: How People Cope with Persuasion Attempts</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Marian Friestad; Peter Wright (1994) - Peer-reviewed theory paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Explains the mental guard that activates when people notice “someone is trying to persuade me.”</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Theory paper; requires application judgment</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/00913367.2005.10639199'><strong>Ad Skepticism: The Consequences of Disbelief</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Carl Obermiller; Eric Spangenberg; Douglas L. MacLachlan (2005) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Directly addresses your instinct to disbelieve ad claims.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic; specific measures of skepticism</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nielsen.com/insights/2021/beyond-martech-building-trust-with-consumers-and-engaging-where-sentiment-is-high/'><strong>Beyond Martech: Building Trust with Consumers and Engaging Where Sentiment Is High</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen (2021) - Industry research / trust in advertising - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows relative trust in word-of-mouth vs paid ad channels.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Measurement company; still useful for channel-trust comparisons</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0969698918307926'><strong>The Effect of Characteristics of Source Credibility on Consumer Behaviour: A Meta-Analysis</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Elvira Ismagilova et al. (2020) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Explains why expertise, trustworthiness, and similarity affect adoption of messages.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Academic meta-analysis; mostly eWOM contexts</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2543925123000323'><strong>How Online Reviews Affect Purchase Intention: A Meta-Analysis Across Contextual and Cultural Factors</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Keda Qiu; Liyi Zhang (2024) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Reviews are a major trust layer after the ad earns inspection.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Review literature heterogeneity; still strong source</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://cris.maastrichtuniversity.nl/ws/files/65443620/mahr_2015_unraveling_the_personalization_paradox.pdf'><strong>Unraveling the Personalization Paradox</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Elizabeth Aguirre; Dominik Mahr; Dhruv Grewal; Ko de Ruyter; Martin Wetzels (2015) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Shows personalization can help or backfire depending on perceived data collection.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Experimental settings; implications depend on current privacy norms</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.econstor.eu/bitstream/10419/325948/1/10.1080_23311975.2023.2296686.pdf'><strong>Meta-Analytic Review of Online Purchase Intention</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Moumita Ghosh et al. (2024) - Peer-reviewed meta-analysis - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Good broad map of online purchase-intention drivers.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Broad e-commerce scope; not SaaS-specific</p></div><h2>Software adoption</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.jstor.org/stable/249008'><strong>Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Fred D. Davis (1989) - Peer-reviewed paper - <span class='priority'>Essential</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Core SaaS adoption equation: useful + easy to use.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Classic model; later models add context, norms, and habit</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://open.ncl.ac.uk/theory-library/technology-acceptance-model.pdf'><strong>Technology Acceptance Model Overview</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>TheoryHub / Newcastle University (2025) - Theory overview - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Readable overview of TAM before/after the original Davis paper.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Secondary summary</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://tilisathibodeaux.com/wordpress/wp-content/uploads/2016/03/Sahin.pdf'><strong>Detailed Review of Rogers&#x27; Diffusion of Innovations Theory</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Ismail Sahin (2006) - Academic review paper - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Adoption categories, perceived attributes of innovation, uncertainty reduction.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Secondary review of Rogers; education-tech angle</p></div><h2>UX and credibility</h2><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nngroup.com/articles/trustworthy-design/'><strong>Trustworthiness in Web Design: 4 Credibility Factors</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen Norman Group (2016) - UX research article - <span class='priority'>High</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful design checklist for landing pages and SaaS websites.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> UX consultancy; practical rather than academic</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ecommerce-improvements/'><strong>Ecommerce Usability Improvements</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen Norman Group (2011) - UX research article - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Useful reminder that content and usability failures block purchases.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> E-commerce focus; translate carefully to SaaS</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://www.nngroup.com/reports/ecommerce-ux-trust-and-credibility/'><strong>Ecommerce User Experience: Trust and Credibility</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Nielsen Norman Group (Current edition) - Paid UX report - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Detailed trust/credibility recommendations if you want deep UX reading.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> Paid consultancy report; valuable if accessible</p></div><div class='source'><div><a href='https://baymard.com/research'><strong>Checkout UX / Ecommerce UX Research</strong></a></div><div class='meta'>Baymard Institute (Current) - UX benchmark/research - <span class='priority'>Useful</span></div><p><strong>Why read:</strong> Very practical for checkout friction, forms, pricing pages, and trust.</p><p><strong>Bias note:</strong> UX research company; credible but commercial</p></div></body></html>
```

---

## File: `05_mindset_model.md`

# Mindset Model: How to Think About Ads, Buyers, and Software Purchases

## 1. Replace the broken model

Bad model:

> Ad makes claim -> person believes claim -> person buys.

Better model:

> Ad creates relevance -> buyer inspects evidence -> risk drops -> buyer tries -> product experience creates belief -> purchase or expansion happens.

The ad is not the courtroom. It is the summons. A skeptical buyer does not need to believe the ad. They only need to think the next step is worth inspecting.

## 2. Buyer states

A buyer is usually in one of these states:

1. **Unaware:** no felt problem. Paid ads mostly fail unless they create category awareness or provoke recognition.
2. **Problem-aware:** knows the pain but not the solution class. Good content names the pain better than the buyer can.
3. **Solution-aware:** knows the category. Needs comparison, evidence, and specificity.
4. **Vendor-aware:** knows you exist. Needs trust, pricing clarity, proof, and risk reduction.
5. **Active buyer:** has urgency, budget, and a decision process. Needs a short path, credible proof, and low friction.

## 3. Purchase equation

Use this mental formula:

> Purchase likelihood = problem intensity x perceived value x trust x ease x timing - perceived risk.

Weak marketers obsess over the CTA. Strong operators improve the whole equation.

## 4. The skeptical buyer rule

A skeptical buyer rejects borrowed belief:

> “I believe it because the ad said it.”

They accept earned belief:

> “I believe it because the evidence survived inspection.”

Your job is to build inspection paths: demo, docs, comparison, independent reviews, transparent pricing, real examples, trial, security posture, limitations, and cancellation clarity.

## 5. Ads that work on skeptical buyers

They tend to do at least one of these:

- Name a problem with unusual precision.
- Show a concrete before/after transformation.
- Offer a low-risk test.
- Route to independent proof.
- Demonstrate the product instead of describing it.
- Reveal a hidden cost the buyer already suspects.
- Respect autonomy: no forced demos, no fake scarcity, no hidden price traps.

## 6. Software-specific belief creation

For digital/software products, belief is often created after the click:

- Interactive demo
- Public docs
- Clear changelog
- Pricing table
- Integration list
- Security page
- Real screenshots
- Sandbox or trial
- Data export and cancellation clarity
- Case studies with constraints and numbers
- Reviews and comparisons

## 7. Your new operating stance

Do not ask: “How do I make people buy?”

Ask:

> “How do I identify people already near a buying state, understand the job they need done, prove the product reduces risk or effort, and make the next step obvious?”

That frame is more ethical and more effective.

---

## File: `06_practical_exercises.md`

# Practical Exercises

## Daily/weekly research rituals

### 1. The 100-ad teardown

Over 6 months, collect and analyze 100 ads. For each ad, record:

- Product/category
- Buyer state targeted: unaware, problem-aware, solution-aware, vendor-aware, active buyer
- Hook type: pain, gain, fear, curiosity, proof, comparison, demo, urgency, offer
- Claim made
- Evidence supplied inside the ad
- What the click leads to
- What skepticism the ad triggers
- What proof would be needed to earn inspection
- Method used
- Buyer state targeted
- Mindset the advertiser seems to hold
- Does the ad ask for belief or inspection?
- What risk does it reduce?
- What risk does it ignore?

### 2. The “show me” rewrite

Take 20 vague ads and rewrite them so they ask for less belief.

Bad: “AI-powered platform that saves teams hours.”

Better: “Paste one messy support email. See it converted into a prioritized Linear issue with customer, urgency, and repro steps.”

### 3. SaaS proof-stack teardown

For 10 SaaS websites, score from 1-5:

- Clear ICP/persona
- Specific problem
- Specific outcome
- Demo quality
- Pricing transparency
- Real screenshots
- Independent proof/reviews
- Security/compliance trust
- Integration clarity
- Trial/onboarding path
- Cancellation/data export clarity

### 4. Buyer interview drill

Ask these questions:

1. What happened that made you start looking?
2. What did you try before?
3. What nearly stopped you from buying?
4. What evidence did you trust?
5. Who else influenced the decision?
6. What did you fear would go wrong?
7. What made the winning product feel safer?
8. What did the vendor say that you ignored?
9. What did the product show that you believed?
10. What would have made you buy faster?

### 5. Landing page rebuild

Pick one product and write three landing page versions:

- Version A: high-intent search visitor
- Version B: skeptical technical buyer
- Version C: manager/business buyer

For each version, change the headline, proof, CTA, screenshots, objections, and risk reversal.

### 6. Measurement discipline drill

For every campaign idea, write:

- Hypothesis
- Target buyer state
- Primary metric
- Guardrail metric
- What would happen without the campaign
- How to test incrementality
- Minimum sample/traffic assumptions
- False-positive risk
- Decision rule

### 7. Ethics audit

For every funnel, ask:

- Is pricing clear before commitment?
- Can users cancel without disproportionate friction?
- Is urgency real?
- Are limitations clear?
- Are testimonials representative?
- Are defaults in the user’s interest?
- Are we exploiting confusion, fatigue, anxiety, or inattention?
- Would we be comfortable explaining this design to a regulator or to the buyer?


## Method-layer exercises

Use `15_methods_playbook.md` and `18_method_practice_drills.md` to practice method selection.

For every campaign you study or design, write:

- Buyer state
- Missing force: attention, relevance, belief, ability, risk, timing, or stakeholder alignment
- Method chosen
- Why this method fits
- Why 2 other tempting methods are wrong
- Proof path after the ad or message
- Mindset to hold
- Primary metric
- Guardrail metric
- Ethics risk

# Generative AI practice extension

Use `32_ai_practice_drills.md` for the full list. Minimum required AI drills:

1. **Skeptical buyer audit:** rewrite 20 ads so they earn inspection rather than demand belief.
2. **Review mining:** use AI to extract buyer language from 100 reviews, then manually validate 20%.
3. **Proof architecture:** map every claim on a landing page to proof or block the claim.
4. **AI-search audit:** ask AI/search tools category questions and record cited sources.
5. **Claims audit:** classify 30 AI product claims by risk and rewrite 10.
6. **Chatbot red-team:** design and attack a buyer concierge bot.
7. **Human-AI creative comparison:** compare human-only, AI-only, and human-AI collaboration output.
8. **Governance packet:** build a claim ledger, experiment ledger, disclosure decision tree, and QA checklist.

The output should be concrete artifacts, not notes.

---

## File: `07_software_saas_playbook.md`

# Software and SaaS Buying Playbook

## 1. The buying surface is bigger than the ad

For SaaS, the buying system includes:

- Ad/search/social touchpoint
- Website and landing page
- Pricing page
- Demo or product tour
- Docs and API reference
- Security/compliance page
- Reviews and comparison pages
- Trial signup
- Onboarding flow
- Activation moment
- Lifecycle emails
- Sales/support touchpoints
- Billing and cancellation

Every one of these can increase or destroy trust.

## 2. Proof stack for a skeptical buyer

### Product proof

- Demo using realistic data
- Interactive sandbox
- Screenshots of exact workflows
- Performance/latency examples
- Clear limitations and non-use-cases

### Social proof

- Reviews from similar users
- Case studies with numbers and constraints
- Public customers if legitimate
- Community mentions
- Peer recommendations

### Technical proof

- Docs
- API examples
- Changelog
- Status page
- Security whitepaper
- Integration guides
- Data import/export paths

### Commercial proof

- Transparent pricing
- Trial/freemium path
- Easy cancellation
- Refund/guarantee where appropriate
- Procurement/security material for B2B

## 3. Match message to buyer state

| Buyer state | Their question | Better asset | Weak asset |
|---|---|---|---|
| Problem-aware | “Is this actually my problem?” | Pain-specific article, calculator, diagnostic | Generic product pitch |
| Solution-aware | “Which approach is best?” | Comparison, use-case guide, demo | Brand slogan |
| Vendor-aware | “Can I trust this vendor?” | Reviews, docs, pricing, security, trial | Forced sales call |
| Active buyer | “Can I decide safely?” | Clear ROI, implementation plan, stakeholder proof | Vague feature list |

## 4. SaaS conversion is not just copy

Common blockers:

- The buyer cannot understand what the product does within 10 seconds.
- The product claims an outcome but does not show the mechanism.
- Pricing is hidden.
- Screenshots are fake-looking or absent.
- Signup is too early relative to trust.
- Trial users do not reach the activation moment.
- The product asks for too many permissions before value is clear.
- Cancellation or data export is unclear.
- The buyer cannot explain the product internally to stakeholders.

## 5. The activation principle

A trial does not convert because it exists. It converts when the user reaches a meaningful state of experienced value.

Define activation as a concrete event, not a vague feeling.

Examples:

- Imported first dataset
- Connected first integration
- Created first report
- Invited first teammate
- Automated first workflow
- Exported first deliverable
- Resolved first support ticket

## 6. The pricing principle

Pricing is positioning. It tells the buyer:

- Who this is for
- What value dimension matters
- How risk is distributed
- How usage will scale
- Whether adoption will be punished
- Whether the company is transparent

For skeptical buyers, hidden pricing can be a trust failure, not just a sales tactic.

---

## File: `08_measurement_and_experimentation.md`

# Measurement and Experimentation Notes

## 1. Metrics are not truth

Clicks, CTR, CPC, CPA, ROAS, CAC, and trial conversion are useful but dangerous. They can all look good while failing to create incremental profit.

The key question:

> What happened because of this marketing that would not have happened otherwise?

That is incrementality.

## 2. Attribution vs incrementality

Attribution assigns credit to visible touchpoints.

Incrementality estimates causal lift.

Example: a buyer searches your brand after hearing about you from a colleague, clicks your branded search ad, and buys. Last-click attribution credits the ad. Incrementality asks whether the buyer would have bought anyway.

## 3. When to distrust reported ROAS

Be skeptical when:

- The channel captures existing demand, especially branded search.
- There is no holdout or geo/user-level experiment.
- Conversion windows are long or arbitrary.
- Platform-reported conversions overlap across channels.
- Retargeting gets credit for buyers already close to buying.
- Organic, referral, and paid paths interact.
- Revenue is volatile and confidence intervals are ignored.

## 4. Minimum experiment template

- Hypothesis:
- Target audience:
- Treatment:
- Control:
- Primary outcome:
- Guardrail outcomes:
- Randomization unit:
- Exclusion rules:
- Duration:
- Minimum detectable effect:
- Decision rule:
- Risk if wrong:

## 5. Good marketing tests

Good tests isolate a decision. Bad tests mix targeting, message, landing page, offer, and timing all at once, then pretend they know what worked.

## 6. Use qualitative and quantitative evidence together

Quantitative data tells you what changed. Qualitative research tells you why the buyer cared, what they feared, and what they believed.

---

## File: `09_ethics_dark_patterns.md`

# Ethics, Dark Patterns, and Boundaries

## 1. Persuasion vs manipulation

Ethical persuasion helps a buyer act on their real preference with more clarity, confidence, and control.

Manipulation steers a buyer toward your desired outcome by hiding cost, increasing confusion, exploiting fatigue, or making alternatives harder to choose.

## 2. Red-line tactics

Avoid:

- Fake scarcity
- Fake countdowns
- Hidden fees
- Preselected paid add-ons that are not clearly disclosed
- Forced continuity without clear cancellation
- Making cancellation much harder than signup
- Confusing opt-outs
- Disguised ads
- Misleading testimonials
- Burying material limitations
- Ambiguous AI claims
- Forced sales calls when basic pricing or product facts could be disclosed

## 3. Safer alternatives

Instead of fake scarcity: explain real capacity, implementation windows, or support constraints.

Instead of hidden pricing: show pricing ranges, starting prices, or a clear pricing logic.

Instead of forced continuity: provide clear reminders before billing.

Instead of dark cancellation: make cancellation easy and use exit surveys to learn.

Instead of exaggerated claims: show real workflow examples and limitations.

## 4. The regulator test

Before shipping a growth tactic, ask:

- Would this look deceptive if screenshotted in a regulator report?
- Would a reasonable buyer understand what they are agreeing to?
- Does this design create value or exploit inattention?
- Are we increasing useful ease or harmful sludge?
- Could a vulnerable user be disproportionately harmed?

## 5. Long-term operator rule

Do not trade durable trust for short-term conversion. In software, retention, expansion, referrals, and reputation often matter more than first-click conversion.

---

## File: `100_model_routing_and_cost_plan.md`

# Model Routing and Cost Plan

## Principle

Use the most capable model to design and verify. Use cheaper/faster models for narrow production once outputs are constrained.

## Model task split

| Task | Model type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy synthesis | Frontier reasoning model | High ambiguity, many constraints |
| Claim extraction | Strong structured-output model | Accuracy and schema adherence |
| Copy variants | Fast creative model | Volume |
| Policy review | Strong reasoning model + rules | Low tolerance for false negatives |
| Data cleaning | Cheap model or deterministic code | Repeatable low-risk work |
| Image prompts | Creative multimodal model | Visual specificity |
| Landing page outline | Strong writing/reasoning model | Structure and trust logic |
| Platform payload generation | Structured-output model | Schema correctness |
| Budget/rule execution | Deterministic code | Do not let LLM improvise money decisions |

## Routing ladder

1. Start with strongest model for the whole workflow to establish quality.
2. Build eval set.
3. Move low-risk subtasks to cheaper models.
4. Keep compliance, claim review, and final launch checks on stronger models plus deterministic validators.
5. Audit cost per launched experiment, not cost per generated variant.

## Cost metrics

- Cost per research memo.
- Cost per approved angle.
- Cost per approved ad variant.
- Cost per launched experiment.
- Cost per learning memo.
- Cost per activated trial/customer.

## Failure rule

If cheaper models increase policy failures, hallucinated claims, or review time, they are not cheaper.

## Mindset

Cost optimisation begins after reliability exists.

---

## File: `101_database_schema_sql.md`

# Database Schema

Converted from `101_database_schema.sql` so the pack remains Markdown-only.

```sql
-- Autonomous Ad Factory database schema
-- Designed as a starting point for PostgreSQL.

CREATE TABLE products (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  category TEXT,
  icp TEXT,
  primary_job TEXT,
  pricing_model TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE claims (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id TEXT REFERENCES products(id),
  raw_claim TEXT NOT NULL,
  approved_wording TEXT NOT NULL,
  prohibited_wording TEXT,
  risk_level TEXT CHECK (risk_level IN ('low','medium','high','blocked')),
  proof_type TEXT,
  proof_url TEXT,
  required_disclaimer TEXT,
  jurisdiction TEXT,
  expires_at DATE,
  status TEXT CHECK (status IN ('draft','evidence_pending','approved','approved_with_disclaimer','expired','blocked')),
  owner TEXT,
  approver TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE buyer_insights (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id TEXT REFERENCES products(id),
  quote TEXT,
  source_type TEXT,
  buyer_state TEXT,
  persona TEXT,
  theme TEXT,
  objection TEXT,
  desired_proof TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE hypotheses (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  product_id TEXT REFERENCES products(id),
  buyer_state TEXT,
  persona TEXT,
  angle TEXT,
  offer TEXT,
  channel TEXT,
  primary_metric TEXT,
  guardrail_metrics TEXT,
  status TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE assets (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  hypothesis_id TEXT REFERENCES hypotheses(id),
  asset_type TEXT,
  channel TEXT,
  copy_json JSONB,
  creative_brief JSONB,
  claim_ids TEXT,
  policy_status TEXT,
  risk_score INTEGER,
  created_by TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE experiments (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  hypothesis_id TEXT REFERENCES hypotheses(id),
  platform TEXT,
  campaign_name TEXT,
  budget_cap NUMERIC,
  start_date DATE,
  end_date DATE,
  primary_metric TEXT,
  decision_rule TEXT,
  kill_rule TEXT,
  status TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE platform_objects (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  experiment_id TEXT REFERENCES experiments(id),
  platform TEXT,
  platform_campaign_id TEXT,
  platform_adset_adgroup_id TEXT,
  platform_ad_id TEXT,
  payload JSONB,
  status TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE metrics_daily (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  experiment_id TEXT REFERENCES experiments(id),
  platform TEXT,
  date DATE,
  impressions INTEGER,
  clicks INTEGER,
  spend NUMERIC,
  conversions INTEGER,
  activated_trials INTEGER,
  pipeline_value NUMERIC,
  revenue NUMERIC,
  raw_metrics JSONB
);

CREATE TABLE approvals (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  object_type TEXT,
  object_id TEXT,
  approver TEXT,
  decision TEXT,
  comments TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

CREATE TABLE learning_memos (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  experiment_id TEXT REFERENCES experiments(id),
  result TEXT,
  what_worked TEXT,
  what_failed TEXT,
  what_not_to_conclude TEXT,
  next_test TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
```

---

## File: `102_backend_orchestration_service.md`

# Backend Orchestration Service

## Service responsibilities

The backend should coordinate agents, tools, approvals, policies, platform APIs, analytics, and learning memory.

## Core services

| Service | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| ProductTruthService | Stores product, feature, claim, proof, limitation data |
| BuyerIntelService | Ingests and structures VOC/research data |
| HypothesisService | Creates angle/offer/channel hypotheses |
| CreativeService | Generates copy, briefs, and asset prompts |
| PolicyService | Validates claims, platform risk, and landing-page consistency |
| ExperimentService | Creates experiments, arms, decision rules |
| PlatformAdapterService | Converts internal campaign specs into platform-specific payloads |
| ApprovalService | Routes decisions to human owners |
| BudgetGuardService | Enforces caps, pacing, kill switches |
| MetricsService | Pulls platform and warehouse metrics |
| LearningService | Writes learning memos and updates priors |

## API endpoints

```text
POST /products
POST /claims
POST /buyer-insights/ingest
POST /hypotheses/generate
POST /assets/generate
POST /policy/review
POST /experiments/create
POST /experiments/{id}/launch
POST /experiments/{id}/pause
GET  /experiments/{id}/metrics
POST /experiments/{id}/learning-memo
```

## Execution graph

```text
Product Brief -> Claim Bank -> Buyer Intel -> Hypotheses -> Creative Drafts -> Policy Gate -> Approval -> Platform Payload -> Launch -> Metrics -> Learning Memo -> Next Hypotheses
```

## Tool permissions

Separate tools into:

- read-only tools,
- draft-only tools,
- review tools,
- write tools,
- destructive tools.

Only deterministic services should execute destructive actions such as pausing campaigns or changing budget.

## Mindset

LLMs reason and draft. Services enforce reality.

---

## File: `103_queue_worker_and_scheduler.md`

# Queue Worker and Scheduler

## Why this matters

Ad factories run on recurring work: fetch metrics, check spend, refresh variants, run policy scans, update stale claims, and produce learning memos.

## Jobs

| Job | Frequency | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `fetch_platform_metrics` | hourly/daily | Pull metrics from APIs |
| `check_budget_pacing` | hourly | Compare spend to caps |
| `run_kill_rules` | hourly/daily | Pause bad/risky variants |
| `scan_policy_status` | daily | Detect disapprovals and risky assets |
| `refresh_creative_candidates` | weekly | Generate new variants from learning memory |
| `expire_claims` | daily | Mark expired claims as unusable |
| `sync_crm_outcomes` | daily | Pull pipeline/revenue outcomes |
| `write_learning_memos` | after test | Summarise result and next test |
| `reconcile_platform_ids` | daily | Ensure internal/external object mapping |

## Queue priorities

1. Safety/kill-switch jobs.
2. Budget-control jobs.
3. Policy review jobs.
4. Metrics ingestion.
5. Creative generation.
6. Research tasks.

## Worker rule

A worker may never launch a campaign unless it receives:

- approved experiment ID,
- approved claim IDs,
- budget cap,
- channel cap,
- approval token or auto-launch policy match,
- valid landing page,
- UTM convention.

## Example scheduler

```yaml
jobs:
  fetch_metrics:
    cron: "0 * * * *"
  budget_guard:
    cron: "15 * * * *"
  kill_rules:
    cron: "30 * * * *"
  claim_expiry:
    cron: "0 4 * * *"
  weekly_learning_review:
    cron: "0 9 * * MON"
```

---

## File: `104_frontend_dashboard_spec.md`

# Frontend Dashboard Specification

## Main screens

### 1. Control Tower

Shows:

- live spend vs cap,
- experiments active,
- blocked assets,
- approvals waiting,
- policy incidents,
- top learnings,
- kill-switch status.

### 2. Claim Bank

Shows:

- approved claims,
- expired claims,
- blocked claims,
- proof links,
- jurisdictions,
- owners,
- required disclaimers.

### 3. Hypothesis Board

Columns:

- backlog,
- drafted,
- policy review,
- approved,
- launched,
- learning complete,
- archived.

### 4. Creative Lab

For each variant:

- copy,
- asset preview,
- buyer state,
- angle,
- offer,
- claim IDs,
- risk score,
- landing page,
- approval status.

### 5. Experiment Monitor

Shows:

- spend,
- CTR,
- CVR,
- CPA,
- activated trials,
- pipeline,
- revenue,
- guardrails,
- decision status.

### 6. Learning Memory

Searchable lessons:

- what worked,
- for whom,
- on which channel,
- with what offer,
- what proof,
- under what constraints.

## Critical UI rule

The dashboard must make it impossible to confuse:

- generated,
- approved,
- launched,
- winning,
- incrementally proven.

## Status labels

Use strict labels:

- Draft
- Policy blocked
- Needs proof
- Needs approval
- Launch-ready
- Active
- Paused by kill rule
- Completed: winner
- Completed: loser
- Completed: inconclusive
- Incrementality proven
- Not incrementality-tested

## Mindset

The dashboard is an accountability surface, not a vanity metrics board.

---

## File: `105_agent_memory_and_learning_system.md`

# Agent Memory and Learning System

## Purpose

The factory should not relearn the same thing every week. It needs durable memory.

## Memory types

### Factual memory

- product facts,
- claims,
- proof,
- limitations,
- pricing,
- platform IDs.

### Strategic memory

- positioning decisions,
- rejected angles,
- ICP definitions,
- category beliefs,
- key objections.

### Experimental memory

- hypotheses,
- test designs,
- results,
- confidence,
- next actions.

### Creative memory

- winning hooks,
- losing hooks,
- fatigue patterns,
- effective visual metaphors,
- banned styles.

## Learning memo fields

```json
{
  "experiment_id": "EXP-001",
  "hypothesis": "Permission preview increases trial starts among privacy-sensitive visitors",
  "result": "inconclusive",
  "confidence": "low",
  "primary_metric": "activated_trials_per_1000_clicks",
  "what_worked": "Higher demo completion",
  "what_failed": "No lift in signup completion",
  "what_not_to_conclude": "Do not conclude privacy proof is unimportant; traffic may have been too cold.",
  "next_test": "Move permission preview below a shorter product demo"
}
```

## Memory retrieval rules

Before generating an ad, retrieve:

- relevant product claims,
- prior tests for same buyer state,
- prior tests for same objection,
- channel-specific constraints,
- active brand rules,
- recent fatigue patterns.

## Staleness rules

Mark memories stale when:

- product changed,
- pricing changed,
- claim expired,
- platform policy changed,
- channel algorithm changed materially,
- audience changed,
- result is older than agreed window.

## Mindset

The point of autonomy is compounding learning, not compounding random generation.

---

## File: `106_b2b_saas_specific_playbooks.md`

# B2B SaaS Autonomous Ads Playbook

## Buyer realities

B2B SaaS buyers are often not one person. They involve users, economic buyers, technical reviewers, security, procurement, and internal champions.

## Core buyer objections

- Will this solve our exact use case?
- Will implementation be painful?
- Can we trust the vendor?
- Is our data safe?
- Will the team adopt it?
- Does it integrate with our stack?
- Will it create more admin work?
- Can I justify budget?
- What happens if it fails?

## Ad paths by buyer state

### Problem-aware

Best messages:

- hidden cost,
- manual workflow pain,
- operational risk,
- visibility gap.

Best proof:

- before/after workflow,
- calculator,
- benchmark of internal waste.

### Solution-aware

Best messages:

- mechanism,
- integration,
- implementation path,
- category comparison.

Best proof:

- demo,
- docs,
- comparison page,
- template.

### Vendor-aware

Best messages:

- why us,
- risk reversal,
- security,
- specific case study,
- pricing clarity.

Best proof:

- case study,
- security page,
- testimonials,
- sandbox,
- technical demo.

## SaaS page modules

- Integration list.
- Security documentation.
- Permission scopes.
- Implementation timeline.
- Data export/cancellation policy.
- Use-case-specific demo.
- Pricing page.
- Competitive alternative page.

## SaaS metrics

Do not optimise only for leads.

Track:

- activated trials,
- qualified pipeline,
- sales-accepted opportunities,
- time to first value,
- expansion signals,
- churn by acquisition source,
- payback period,
- CAC by channel,
- sales cycle length.

## Mindset

In B2B SaaS, an ad rarely closes the purchase. It starts or accelerates a trust-building sequence.

---

## File: `107_ecommerce_specific_playbooks.md`

# Ecommerce Autonomous Ads Playbook

## Why include this

Even if your focus is software, ecommerce teaches direct-response mechanics: offers, bundles, urgency, creative testing, landing-page consistency, conversion friction, and product proof.

## Use when

- product is simple,
- buyer can decide quickly,
- price is visible,
- fulfilment is reliable,
- creative can show concrete product use.

## Core levers

- product demonstration,
- offer clarity,
- shipping/returns proof,
- reviews,
- bundles,
- comparison,
- use cases,
- creator content,
- retargeting.

## Guardrails

- no fake scarcity,
- no misleading discounts,
- no fake reviews,
- no hidden shipping costs,
- no unrealistic results,
- ensure offer availability.

## Useful transfer to SaaS

- Clear CTA beats clever copy.
- Proof beats promises.
- Friction kills intent.
- Post-click experience matters.
- Creative fatigue is real.
- Measurement can lie if attribution is over-trusted.

---

## File: `108_local_services_specific_playbooks.md`

# Local Services Autonomous Ads Playbook

## Why include this

Local services sharpen intent routing: location, urgency, trust, availability, and proof. These concepts also transfer to software when buyers have urgent operational pain.

## Common buyer states

- urgent need,
- comparing providers,
- checking trust,
- price anxiety,
- availability constraint.

## Useful methods

- search ads for urgent intent,
- review-led landing pages,
- service-area pages,
- call tracking,
- booking CTAs,
- retargeting with proof,
- localised copy.

## Guardrails

- accurate service areas,
- current availability,
- no misleading “near me” pages,
- no fake reviews,
- clear pricing/disclaimers.

## Transfer to SaaS

Local services teach the value of immediate relevance. For SaaS, this becomes:

- role-specific page,
- use-case-specific demo,
- integration-specific page,
- problem-specific landing page.

---

## File: `109_ad_library_competitor_research.md`

# Competitor Ad Library Research Workflow

## Objective

Use public ad libraries and competitive surfaces to learn what the market is saying, not to copy blindly.

## Research fields

For each competitor ad:

- platform,
- date seen,
- ad format,
- hook,
- buyer state,
- persona,
- pain,
- promised outcome,
- proof type,
- offer,
- CTA,
- landing page,
- claim risk,
- visual pattern,
- differentiation opportunity.

## Analysis questions

1. What problems are competitors repeatedly naming?
2. What claims are unsupported or vague?
3. What proof are they missing?
4. What objections are they ignoring?
5. What landing page modules are common?
6. Which channels are used for which buyer states?
7. What could we say truthfully that they cannot?

## Output

```json
{
  "category_patterns": ["AI automation", "time savings", "team productivity"],
  "overused_claims": ["10x productivity", "work smarter"],
  "proof_gaps": ["few permission/security demos", "few limitation statements"],
  "opportunities": ["technical sandbox", "honest AI limitations", "integration-specific workflows"]
}
```

## Mindset

Competitor research is not theft. It is category literacy.

---
