```html
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The product experience is weak, reviews are incentivized without transparency, or support cannot handle criticism.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Buyers distrust brand claims but inspect other buyers&#x27; experiences, especially when reviews are specific and balanced.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Earned reputation. Make real experience visible.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Ask 20 successful users for specific reviews using prompts about use case and outcome.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Fake reviews, undisclosed incentives, suppressing negative feedback.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M14 - Referral / word-of-mouth systems</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Trust and acquisition</span><span class="pill">Problem-aware through active buyer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Users are satisfied, the product is shareable, and a recommendation reduces adoption risk.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Retention is weak, the product is embarrassing/private, or incentives distort quality.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> People trust peers more than ads. Referrals borrow trust from existing relationships.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Make successful users generous and specific.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Add referral prompt after a successful outcome, not at signup.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Dark social spam, undisclosed incentives, aggressive contact importing.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M15 - Community-led growth</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Trust, education, retention</span><span class="pill">Problem-aware, solution-aware, user/adopter</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> The problem space benefits from shared learning, identity, practice, templates, or peer support.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You want a cheap acquisition hack, cannot moderate, or the product does not support ongoing discussion.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Communities build familiarity, peer proof, and category belief over time.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Host the problem space, not a billboard for your product.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Run a 4-week cohort or office-hours series around one painful workflow.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Astroturfing; hiding commercial intent; manipulating discussions.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M16 - Founder-led / expert-led content</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Authority and trust</span><span class="pill">Unaware through vendor-aware</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> The buyer values technical credibility, category insight, strong opinions, or behind-the-scenes competence.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The founder lacks useful insight, content becomes ego performance, or claims are not backed by product reality.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Visible expertise can reduce trust gaps, especially for technical or emerging categories.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Operator explaining the truth of the market.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Publish 6 technical/product essays answering objections heard in sales or support.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Cult-of-personality manipulation; overstating expertise.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M17 - Webinars / workshops / live demos</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Education and sales enablement</span><span class="pill">Problem-aware, solution-aware, vendor-aware</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> The buyer needs education, stakeholder alignment, or a guided view of a complex workflow.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The product is simple enough for self-serve, or you will deliver a disguised sales pitch with little value.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Live format creates attention, interaction, and contextual proof that static pages cannot.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Help them make the decision smarter.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Host one practical workshop around a painful job, with product demo only after teaching the decision criteria.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Bait-and-switch education into pressure selling.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M18 - Sales-assisted demo and discovery</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Complex conversion</span><span class="pill">Vendor-aware, active buyer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Price, risk, implementation, or stakeholder complexity is high enough that self-serve proof is insufficient.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Buyers clearly prefer self-serve and the salesperson adds friction instead of clarity.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> A good sales process turns general proof into buyer-specific confidence.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Consultant and risk reducer. Diagnose before demonstrating.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Redesign demo flow to start with buyer job and objection map, not feature tour.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Pressure tactics, false scarcity, hiding implementation cost.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M19 - Account-based marketing (ABM)</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Enterprise demand creation/capture</span><span class="pill">Target accounts across problem-aware to active buyer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> A small set of high-value accounts can justify tailored research, messaging, and multi-stakeholder orchestration.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Deal sizes are too small, account data is poor, or you cannot coordinate marketing/sales/customer success.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Enterprise buying is account-level, not just individual-level. ABM aligns proof to stakeholders and account context.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Precision campaign architect. One account is a market.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Pick 20 high-fit accounts and build industry-specific proof + outbound + retargeting + sales follow-up.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Creepy personalization or misuse of personal data.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M20 - Outbound email / LinkedIn</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Demand creation and targeted acquisition</span><span class="pill">Problem-aware or likely-problem accounts</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> You can identify a high-fit buyer and write a relevant, respectful message tied to a real business pain.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You are guessing, blasting lists, making vague claims, or relying on volume to compensate for irrelevance.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Good outbound interrupts only because it is unusually relevant and lowers effort for a buyer who has a real problem.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Earn the interruption.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Send 30 highly researched messages to one ICP with one concrete pain and one proof asset.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Spam, deceptive subject lines, scraping misuse, no opt-out.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M21 - Partner / integration co-marketing</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Trust and distribution</span><span class="pill">Solution-aware, vendor-aware</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Your product depends on or complements another trusted platform, integration, agency, consultant, or ecosystem.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The integration is weak, the partner&#x27;s audience is misaligned, or the partnership is only logo-swapping.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Borrowed context and trusted distribution reduce discovery and trust friction.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Ecosystem builder. Make both audiences more successful.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Create one joint workflow demo with a complementary product and promote to both audiences.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Implying endorsement or integration depth that does not exist.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M22 - Marketplace / app store listing optimization</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Demand capture</span><span class="pill">Solution-aware, active buyer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Buyers search in ecosystems such as Chrome Web Store, Slack/Atlassian/Salesforce/Shopify/HubSpot marketplaces, mobile app stores, or cloud marketplaces.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The category is not marketplace-driven or reviews/support are not ready.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Marketplace search carries high intent and borrowed trust from the platform ecosystem.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Shelf discipline. Make the product instantly inspectable in the buyer&#x27;s chosen store.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Rewrite listing around one primary use case and add a 60-second workflow demo.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Misleading permissions, fake reviews, hiding data access.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M23 - Pricing and packaging strategy</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Conversion and monetization</span><span class="pill">Vendor-aware, active buyer, expansion</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> People understand the product but hesitate on value, plan fit, risk, or procurement.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You are changing price to compensate for unclear value or poor retention.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Pricing communicates who the product is for, what value scales with, and how much risk the buyer carries.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Price is architecture, not a number.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Interview lost deals/trial users about pricing confusion, then simplify plan names and upgrade triggers.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Drip pricing, hidden fees, forced bundles, cancellation sludge.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M24 - Risk reversal and trust infrastructure</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Risk reduction</span><span class="pill">Vendor-aware, active buyer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Buyers worry about wasting money, implementation failure, security, cancellation, data lock-in, or internal embarrassment.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You cannot operationally honor the promise or the product has unresolved reliability issues.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Many buyers are not unconvinced about value; they are afraid of being wrong.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Remove fear honestly.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Add a visible &#x27;What happens if this is not for me?&#x27; section to pricing and checkout.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Making promises hard to exercise; hiding cancellation/export paths.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M25 - Landing page conversion optimization (CRO)</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Conversion</span><span class="pill">All, but especially paid traffic and active buyers</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Qualified traffic arrives but does not inspect, sign up, request demo, or continue.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Traffic quality is poor, sample sizes are too small, or teams want button-color changes instead of value clarity.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> CRO reduces friction, ambiguity, and mismatch between buyer intent and proof path.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Decision architect. Every section must answer a buyer question.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Rewrite the page for one buyer state and one job, then test against the generic page.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Dark patterns, forced action, hidden terms.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M26 - A/B testing and incrementality testing</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Measurement</span><span class="pill">All</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> You need to know whether a change caused improvement rather than merely correlated with it.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Traffic is too small, the decision is low-risk, or you cannot define a meaningful metric.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Experiments reduce self-deception. They reveal whether your method created incremental behavior.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Scientist, not believer.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Run one landing-page or email test with a prewritten decision rule and a guardrail metric.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Experimenting on users without regard to harm or consent where needed.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M27 - Lead magnets and newsletters</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Education and nurture</span><span class="pill">Unaware, problem-aware, solution-aware</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> The buying cycle is long and you can repeatedly provide useful material before purchase.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You use low-value gated PDFs to collect emails from people with no buying intent.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> It creates a permissioned channel for staged trust-building and problem education.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Earn permission with substance.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Create one ungated strong article and offer a related template/calculator as optional follow-up.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Bait-and-switch content, misleading forms, aggressive nurture.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M28 - Influencer / creator / analyst relations</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Trust and reach</span><span class="pill">Unaware through vendor-aware</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Your buyers follow trusted experts, creators, consultants, analysts, or community leaders.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> Influencer fit is weak, disclosure is unclear, or the audience is broad but not qualified.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Credible third parties can create attention and borrowed trust, especially when they demonstrate the product honestly.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Borrow trust carefully; never rent fake belief.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Partner with 3 niche experts for honest workflow demos, not generic endorsements.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Undisclosed sponsorship, scripted fake reviews, inflated claims.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M29 - Launch campaigns / waitlists / beta programs</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Demand creation and validation</span><span class="pill">Unaware, problem-aware, early adopters</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> You need market feedback, early adopters, buzz, or a concentrated proof event.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The product is not ready for scrutiny or the launch will attract the wrong audience.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Launches create a temporary attention window and social signal; betas recruit tolerant early users.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Use attention to learn, not just to announce.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Run a small beta with explicit use-case qualification and structured feedback calls.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> False scarcity, fake social proof, hiding beta limitations.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M30 - Reactivation / winback</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Retention and revenue recovery</span><span class="pill">Dormant lead, inactive trial/user, churned customer</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> People had real intent or value before but stopped due to timing, friction, missing feature, price, or competing priorities.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The user was wrong-fit, churned angrily, or the reason for leaving has not changed.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Prior familiarity lowers discovery cost. A relevant change can reopen the decision.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Respect the previous no. Return only with a changed reason.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Segment churned users by reason and send only the segment whose objection is now solved.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Guilt framing, misleading &#x27;account will close&#x27; messages, hard opt-out.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M31 - Expansion / upsell / cross-sell</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Revenue expansion</span><span class="pill">Successful paid user/team/account</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> The customer has achieved value and now faces scale, collaboration, security, automation, or governance needs.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> The customer has not adopted the core product or upsell would hide the real problem.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Existing trust and usage data reveal the next job better than cold acquisition can.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Expand only after value is proven.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> Identify one usage threshold that predicts successful upgrade and trigger a helpful in-product prompt.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Artificial limits, data hostage tactics, surprise plan changes.</p>
</div>
<div class="card">
<h3>M32 - Customer success as marketing</h3>
<div class="meta"><span class="pill">Retention, expansion, proof creation</span><span class="pill">Paid customer and future buyer references</span></div>
<p><span class="label">Use when:</span> Customers need help reaching outcomes and their success can become proof for similar buyers.</p>
<p><span class="label">Avoid when:</span> You are trying to patch product weakness with manual support forever.</p>
<p><span class="label">Why:</span> Successful customers create retention, expansion, referrals, reviews, and case studies.</p>
<p><span class="label">Mindset:</span> Make the promise true.</p>
<p><span class="label">First experiment:</span> For 10 new customers, create a 30-day outcome plan and ask for proof only after outcome achieved.</p>
<p><span class="label">Ethics risk:</span> Overpromising outcomes in sales and forcing success teams to compensate.</p>
</div>
</div></body></html>
```

---

## File: `21_generative_ai_method_playbook.md`

# Generative AI Method Playbook

This is the deeper tactical layer for using generative AI in digital/software marketing and sales.

The operating rule:

> AI does not create demand by itself. AI increases the speed, range, and personalization of your attempts to understand and serve demand. If your offer, proof, targeting, or measurement is weak, AI mainly helps you make more weak work faster.

Use this file to choose methods by buyer state, risk level, and proof need. Every method has a mindset because the mindset determines whether you use AI as leverage or as a shortcut into lazy manipulation.


## 1. Review mining with LLMs

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You have many reviews, comments, forum posts, support tickets, or competitor reviews.


**Do not use when:** Do not use it as final truth. Use it to find patterns to verify with humans.


**Why it works:** LLMs cluster language, objections, jobs, and emotional triggers faster than manual scanning.


**Mindset:** Be a forensic analyst. Extract buyer language; do not invent it.


**Primary metrics:** Quote frequency, themes found, verified interviews, conversion lift from using real language.


**Failure modes:** Hallucinated quotes, overfitting to loud users, missing silent churn reasons.


**First safety rule:** Ask the model to quote exact source lines and separate evidence from inference.


## 2. Interview guide generation

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You need to run JTBD or buyer interviews and want sharper questions quickly.


**Do not use when:** Do not let AI interview people without supervision for sensitive/high-value research.


**Why it works:** AI can create question ladders, probes, and bias checks from your hypotheses.


**Mindset:** Be a disciplined investigator, not a pitchman.


**Primary metrics:** Interview completion, quality of surprising insights, number of falsified assumptions.


**Failure modes:** Leading questions that confirm your desired answer.


**First safety rule:** Ask AI to rewrite questions to avoid selling, leading, or assuming.


## 3. Transcript coding and theme extraction

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You have call/interview transcripts and need patterns across objections, triggers, and decision criteria.


**Do not use when:** Do not replace human review when stakes are high or sample is small.


**Why it works:** AI accelerates qualitative coding and can compare segments.


**Mindset:** Be a judge weighing evidence.


**Primary metrics:** Intercoder agreement, theme stability, exact quotes, actionability.


**Failure modes:** Overgeneralizing from a few charismatic quotes.


**First safety rule:** Force line citations and keep a human-coded sample.


## 4. Synthetic customer simulation

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You need hypotheses before spending money on interviews or ads.


**Do not use when:** Do not treat synthetic customers as evidence of real demand.


**Why it works:** LLMs can simulate plausible perspectives and expose blind spots, but they reflect training data and prompt framing.


**Mindset:** Be a war-gamer. Use it to prepare, not decide.


**Primary metrics:** Number of hypotheses generated, later validation rate, discarded bad assumptions.


**Failure modes:** Believing fake certainty, persona stereotypes.


**First safety rule:** Label all synthetic output as speculation until validated.


## 5. Competitor review triangulation

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You need to find gaps in competitors’ products, pricing, onboarding, docs, and support.


**Do not use when:** Do not scrape or use private/prohibited data.


**Why it works:** Combines review mining, feature comparison, and objection mapping.


**Mindset:** Be an intelligence analyst.


**Primary metrics:** Repeated competitor complaints, angle quality, proof gaps identified.


**Failure modes:** Cherry-picking weakness while ignoring competitor strengths.


**First safety rule:** Use multiple sources: G2/Capterra/forums/docs/pricing pages.


## 6. Support-ticket opportunity mining

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You have tickets/chats and need upgrade triggers, churn risk, onboarding friction, and feature demand.


**Do not use when:** Do not use private customer data in public AI tools without approval.


**Why it works:** Tickets reveal real friction in buyers' own language.


**Mindset:** Be a product-growth diagnostician.


**Primary metrics:** Ticket categories, support burden reduced, upgrade opportunities, churn themes.


**Failure modes:** Privacy breach, sampling only angry users.


**First safety rule:** De-identify data and cluster by job/problem, not only feature request.


## 7. Intent query clustering

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You have search-console, keyword, paid search, or site-search data.


**Do not use when:** Do not chase keywords unrelated to your ICP or ability to serve.


**Why it works:** AI can cluster by intent stage and map content/ad offers.


**Mindset:** Be a traffic controller.


**Primary metrics:** High-intent clusters, CAC by cluster, demo/trial rate by intent.


**Failure modes:** Keyword volume addiction and low-quality leads.


**First safety rule:** Map every cluster to buyer state and proof asset.


## 8. Market map synthesis

**Family:** Research


**Use when:** You need to understand category structure, alternatives, substitutes, and buyer comparison logic.


**Do not use when:** Do not use unverified AI market maps in investor/customer materials.


**Why it works:** AI can synthesize category players, alternatives, and language fast.


**Mindset:** Be a cartographer.


**Primary metrics:** Completeness, missing alternatives found, stakeholder validation.


**Failure modes:** False competitors, stale data.


**First safety rule:** Ground with public sources and buyer interviews.


## 9. ICP pressure test

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You have an ICP hypothesis and need to sharpen who should and should not buy.


**Do not use when:** Do not broaden ICP because AI found many possible segments.


**Why it works:** AI can stress-test urgency, budget, trigger events, and proof needs.


**Mindset:** Be ruthless. Narrow until the sales motion becomes obvious.


**Primary metrics:** Segment win rate, sales cycle, activation, retention, support load.


**Failure modes:** Attractive but unprofitable segments.


**First safety rule:** Force disqualification criteria.


## 10. JTBD map generation

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You need to connect product features to progress customers want to make.


**Do not use when:** Do not stop at generic pains like 'save time'.


**Why it works:** AI can draft job maps and forces/struggles from research inputs.


**Mindset:** Be a systems mapper.


**Primary metrics:** Clarity of job, interview confirmation, message relevance.


**Failure modes:** Invented jobs detached from real buying moments.


**First safety rule:** Use only real quotes as inputs when possible.


## 11. Positioning hypothesis lab

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You need several positioning options before choosing a market frame.


**Do not use when:** Do not let AI choose positioning by prettiness.


**Why it works:** AI can generate category, enemy, alternative, and differentiated-value frames.


**Mindset:** Be a strategist choosing a battlefield.


**Primary metrics:** Message comprehension, recall, qualified conversion, sales feedback.


**Failure modes:** Me-too claims, impossible-to-prove differentiation.


**First safety rule:** Tie every claim to evidence and buyer alternative.


## 12. Proof architecture design

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You know claims but not what proof must surround them.


**Do not use when:** Do not rely on testimonials when buyers need technical/security proof.


**Why it works:** AI can map claims to evidence types: demo, docs, reviews, ROI, security, case study.


**Mindset:** Be an evidence architect.


**Primary metrics:** Proof completeness, drop-off reduction, sales objections reduced.


**Failure modes:** Claim-proof mismatch.


**First safety rule:** For every claim ask: what would a skeptical buyer inspect next?


## 13. Objection-to-asset matrix

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** Sales hears recurring objections.


**Do not use when:** Do not answer every objection with more copy; sometimes fix product/pricing.


**Why it works:** AI converts objections into needed assets and tests.


**Mindset:** Be a friction eliminator.


**Primary metrics:** Objection frequency, win rate, sales-cycle reduction.


**Failure modes:** Defending weaknesses instead of resolving them.


**First safety rule:** Classify objections: misunderstanding, risk, missing capability, pricing, trust.


## 14. Category narrative builder

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You need to explain why the old way is breaking and why the category matters now.


**Do not use when:** Do not manufacture false urgency or fear.


**Why it works:** AI can generate narrative arcs and evidence needs.


**Mindset:** Be a teacher of change.


**Primary metrics:** Audience comprehension, content engagement, inbound quality.


**Failure modes:** Dramatic but unsupported narratives.


**First safety rule:** Back the narrative with sources and customer reality.


## 15. Pricing-page critique

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You need to reduce pricing confusion and increase confidence.


**Do not use when:** Do not hide fees or manipulate plans.


**Why it works:** AI can inspect clarity, plan boundaries, risk reversal, and upgrade logic.


**Mindset:** Be a risk reducer.


**Primary metrics:** Pricing-page conversion, sales questions, refund/churn, plan-fit accuracy.


**Failure modes:** Dark patterns, bait-and-switch, hidden limits.


**First safety rule:** Require plain-language total cost and who each plan is for.


## 16. AI feature value framing

**Family:** Strategy


**Use when:** You sell AI features and need to avoid AI-washing.


**Do not use when:** Do not lead with 'AI-powered' if the buyer cares about outcome and risk.


**Why it works:** AI can translate technical capability into buyer jobs, limitations, and proof.


**Mindset:** Be concrete: what task changes, how much, and what risk remains?


**Primary metrics:** AI feature adoption, time-to-value, retention, support tickets.


**Failure modes:** Overclaiming autonomous capability.


**First safety rule:** State limitation and human-control boundaries.


## 17. Angle matrix generation

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need many honest ways to frame the same offer for testing.


**Do not use when:** Do not create manipulative fear/shame angles.


**Why it works:** AI can combine jobs, emotions, proof types, and channels into testable angles.


**Mindset:** Be a portfolio manager of hypotheses.


**Primary metrics:** CTR, CVR, lead quality, downstream activation.


**Failure modes:** Volume without insight; repetitive AI slop.


**First safety rule:** Generate angles from real research; kill weak ones fast.


## 18. Hook stress test

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You have ad hooks and need to identify vague, hypey, or unbelievable claims.


**Do not use when:** Do not optimize hooks only for clicks.


**Why it works:** AI can evaluate specificity, inspectability, skepticism, and buyer fit.


**Mindset:** Be skeptical like your buyer.


**Primary metrics:** Hook believability, qualified CTR, bounce rate, comments.


**Failure modes:** Clickbait that poisons trust.


**First safety rule:** Ask: what proof does this hook force us to show?


## 19. Ad variant factory

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need structured ad variants across pain, proof, demo, comparison, and risk-reversal angles.


**Do not use when:** Do not flood channels without a test design.


**Why it works:** AI lowers production cost; testing determines truth.


**Mindset:** Be an experimenter, not a spammer.


**Primary metrics:** Incremental conversions, CAC, creative fatigue, lead quality.


**Failure modes:** Confounded tests and brand dilution.


**First safety rule:** Limit variables: one hypothesis per variant group.


## 20. Landing page proof rewrite

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You have traffic but skeptical visitors are not converting.


**Do not use when:** Do not solve a broken product or wrong audience with copy.


**Why it works:** AI can reorganize pages by buyer questions, proof, objections, and CTA sequence.


**Mindset:** Be a guide through uncertainty.


**Primary metrics:** Scroll depth, demo/trial clicks, conversion rate, sales-fit.


**Failure modes:** Overlong pages with no real proof.


**First safety rule:** Map each section to a buyer question.


## 21. Demo script generation

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need product demos that show the buyer's job, not feature inventory.


**Do not use when:** Do not create fake demos that hide constraints.


**Why it works:** AI can structure demos around before/after workflow and decision criteria.


**Mindset:** Be a show-don't-tell operator.


**Primary metrics:** Demo completion, sales conversion, activation from demo viewers.


**Failure modes:** Demo theatre; no operational reality.


**First safety rule:** Use real workflows and name limitations.


## 22. Video storyboard / UGC concepting

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need creative concepts for short-form or explainer videos.


**Do not use when:** Do not fake customers or endorsements.


**Why it works:** AI can draft scenes, cuts, hooks, and proof beats.


**Mindset:** Be a director of evidence, not spectacle.


**Primary metrics:** View-through, qualified clicks, watch time, trial starts.


**Failure modes:** Synthetic authenticity that feels fake.


**First safety rule:** Anchor every scene in a real job or proof point.


## 23. Founder-led content drafting

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need expertise-rich content that earns trust.


**Do not use when:** Do not let AI manufacture personal experience.


**Why it works:** AI can outline, challenge, and polish; founder must supply judgment and stories.


**Mindset:** Be the expert; AI is the editor.


**Primary metrics:** Engagement by ICP, replies, referrals, branded search.


**Failure modes:** Generic thought leadership.


**First safety rule:** Require specific examples, tradeoffs, failures, and lessons.


## 24. GEO / AI-answer content brief

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need content that answer engines can cite and summarize accurately.


**Do not use when:** Do not stuff keywords or write for bots only.


**Why it works:** AI can structure clear definitions, comparisons, evidence, FAQs, and citations.


**Mindset:** Be a librarian building reliable evidence objects.


**Primary metrics:** AI search citations, referral quality, branded queries, citation share.


**Failure modes:** Thin AI spam, hallucinated claims.


**First safety rule:** Use primary sources, schema, clear authorship, and dated facts.


## 25. Case study builder

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** You need proof assets from customer wins.


**Do not use when:** Do not invent metrics or cherry-pick nonrepresentative success.


**Why it works:** AI can structure problem, context, intervention, result, tradeoffs, and implementation details.


**Mindset:** Be a witness preparing evidence.


**Primary metrics:** Case study influenced pipeline, sales-cycle reduction, objection reduction.


**Failure modes:** Vague success stories with no baseline.


**First safety rule:** Include baseline, constraints, numbers, and buyer quotes.


## 26. Comparison page generator

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** Buyers compare you with alternatives.


**Do not use when:** Do not attack competitors unfairly or hide where they are better.


**Why it works:** AI can organize fair comparison tables, use cases, migration considerations, and fit/no-fit guidance.


**Mindset:** Be a trusted advisor.


**Primary metrics:** Comparison-page conversion, sales assist, AI-search visibility.


**Failure modes:** Biased comparison that destroys trust.


**First safety rule:** Include 'choose competitor when...' sections.


## 27. Documentation-to-marketing repurposing

**Family:** Creative


**Use when:** Technical docs contain buyer proof.


**Do not use when:** Do not turn precise docs into vague slogans.


**Why it works:** AI can turn docs into buyer explainers, diagrams, FAQs, and demo scripts.


**Mindset:** Be a translator, not a simplifier of truth.


**Primary metrics:** Docs-assisted conversion, developer activation, reduced support questions.


**Failure modes:** Loss of technical accuracy.


**First safety rule:** Have SMEs review every technical claim.


## 28. Segment-specific page generation

**Family:** Personalization


**Use when:** You have distinct ICPs with different jobs and proof needs.


**Do not use when:** Do not create thousands of shallow pages.


**Why it works:** AI helps adapt messaging and proof to segment context.


**Mindset:** Be relevant without becoming creepy.


**Primary metrics:** Segment CVR, time on page, pipeline quality.


**Failure modes:** Thin duplicate content, privacy concerns.


**First safety rule:** Use segment-level context, not invasive personal data.


## 29. Account-based message drafting

**Family:** Personalization


**Use when:** You have target accounts and public context.


**Do not use when:** Do not imply knowledge from private surveillance.


**Why it works:** AI can tailor outreach around public triggers, tech stack, initiatives, and likely jobs.


**Mindset:** Be useful, specific, and respectful.


**Primary metrics:** Reply rate, meeting quality, unsubscribe/spam rate.


**Failure modes:** Creepy personalization, hallucinated company facts.


**First safety rule:** Only use verifiable public facts and cite internal sources.


## 30. Lifecycle email sequence design

**Family:** Lifecycle


**Use when:** Users sign up but fail to activate or upgrade.


**Do not use when:** Do not bombard users who have not shown intent.


**Why it works:** AI can map emails to activation events, objections, and milestones.


**Mindset:** Be a coach guiding progress.


**Primary metrics:** Activation, feature adoption, trial-to-paid, unsubscribes.


**Failure modes:** Too much email, low relevance.


**First safety rule:** Trigger from behavior and value milestones.


## 31. In-app nudge copy

**Family:** Lifecycle


**Use when:** Users are in product and need help taking next useful action.


**Do not use when:** Do not interrupt deep work or use guilt/pressure.


**Why it works:** AI can produce concise nudges by state and goal.


**Mindset:** Be a helpful guide inside the task.


**Primary metrics:** Feature adoption, completion rate, nudge dismissal rate.


**Failure modes:** Manipulative interruptions.


**First safety rule:** Use reversible, dismissible, context-aware prompts.


## 32. Churn-risk message generation

**Family:** Lifecycle


**Use when:** A user shows inactivity or downgrade risk.


**Do not use when:** Do not use fear or make cancellation hard.


**Why it works:** AI can tailor help, education, and save offers to likely reason.


**Mindset:** Be a problem solver, not a hostage taker.


**Primary metrics:** Retention lift, save rate, satisfaction after save.


**Failure modes:** Annoying users who want to leave.


**First safety rule:** Make cancellation clear; offer help, data export, or downgrade.


## 33. Customer education content lab

**Family:** Lifecycle


**Use when:** Buyers need to become successful users after purchase.


**Do not use when:** Do not treat education as upsell spam.


**Why it works:** AI can create tutorials, checklists, examples, and role-specific enablement.


**Mindset:** Be the adoption architect.


**Primary metrics:** Time-to-value, completion, support tickets, expansion.


**Failure modes:** Generic content no one uses.


**First safety rule:** Tie content to activation milestones and jobs.


## 34. Sales-call brief generator

**Family:** Sales


**Use when:** A rep needs to understand account context and likely objections quickly.


**Do not use when:** Do not use unverified AI guesses as facts.


**Why it works:** AI summarizes public data, CRM notes, prior calls, and likely agenda.


**Mindset:** Be prepared, not performative.


**Primary metrics:** Call quality, discovery depth, next-step conversion.


**Failure modes:** Hallucinated facts and overpersonalization.


**First safety rule:** Separate verified facts from hypotheses.


## 35. Objection handler / battlecard

**Family:** Sales


**Use when:** Reps need concise answers to competitor, pricing, security, and implementation concerns.


**Do not use when:** Do not script manipulative pressure lines.


**Why it works:** AI can generate answer paths and proof assets.


**Mindset:** Be a calm expert lowering risk.


**Primary metrics:** Win rate in common objection scenarios, rep adoption.


**Failure modes:** Robotic scripts and unsupported claims.


**First safety rule:** Link every answer to proof.


## 36. Proposal / RFP assistant

**Family:** Sales


**Use when:** You need to respond consistently and fast to structured buyer requirements.


**Do not use when:** Do not auto-submit unreviewed answers, especially legal/security claims.


**Why it works:** AI drafts from approved knowledge base and past answers.


**Mindset:** Be exact and compliant.


**Primary metrics:** Response time, error rate, win rate, legal rework.


**Failure modes:** Fabricated commitments.


**First safety rule:** Use retrieval from approved source only and SME review.


## 37. Post-demo follow-up personalization

**Family:** Sales


**Use when:** A buyer attended a demo and needs a useful next step.


**Do not use when:** Do not send generic recap spam.


**Why it works:** AI can map demo topics to proof links, next actions, and stakeholder-specific notes.


**Mindset:** Be a project manager for buyer confidence.


**Primary metrics:** Reply rate, stakeholder forwards, next meeting conversion.


**Failure modes:** Overlong recaps and false promises.


**First safety rule:** Include only what was discussed plus relevant proof.


## 38. Customer success expansion map

**Family:** Sales/CS


**Use when:** You want ethical expansion based on proven usage and unmet jobs.


**Do not use when:** Do not upsell before value is achieved.


**Why it works:** AI can identify account patterns, missing workflows, and expansion hypotheses.


**Mindset:** Be a steward of customer value.


**Primary metrics:** Expansion rate, NRR, satisfaction, usage depth.


**Failure modes:** Pushing features instead of outcomes.


**First safety rule:** Tie expansion to customer goals and demonstrated value.


## 39. Experiment design assistant

**Family:** Measurement


**Use when:** You need to test AI-generated creatives, pages, emails, or workflows.


**Do not use when:** Do not let AI pick winners from underpowered data.


**Why it works:** AI can draft hypotheses, sample needs, guardrails, and interpretation checks.


**Mindset:** Be a scientist of buyer behavior.


**Primary metrics:** Test validity, lift, confidence/credible intervals, downstream quality.


**Failure modes:** Peeking, multiple-comparison false positives.


**First safety rule:** Pre-register hypothesis and success metrics.


## 40. Attribution critique agent

**Family:** Measurement


**Use when:** Your dashboard says a campaign works but you are unsure if it is incremental.


**Do not use when:** Do not equate last-click ROAS with causal lift.


**Why it works:** AI can interrogate measurement assumptions and suggest holdouts/lift tests.


**Mindset:** Be suspicious of convenient numbers.


**Primary metrics:** Incrementality evidence, channel mix quality, CAC payback.


**Failure modes:** Dashboard worship.


**First safety rule:** Ask: what would have happened without this?


## 41. Creative fatigue monitor

**Family:** Measurement


**Use when:** High-performing ads decline over time.


**Do not use when:** Do not react to noise with constant random changes.


**Why it works:** AI can detect pattern shifts and suggest new variants by winning theme.


**Mindset:** Be a portfolio manager.


**Primary metrics:** Frequency, CTR decay, CPA drift, qualitative comments.


**Failure modes:** Over-rotating before statistical signal.


**First safety rule:** Refresh within proven angles before abandoning them.


## 42. Insight extraction from experiments

**Family:** Measurement


**Use when:** You have results but need learning, not just winner/loser.


**Do not use when:** Do not overread tiny differences.


**Why it works:** AI can summarize patterns by segment, channel, and creative angle.


**Mindset:** Be a learning accountant.


**Primary metrics:** Reusable insights, future test quality, documented decisions.


**Failure modes:** Post-hoc storytelling.


**First safety rule:** Separate confirmed, suggestive, and speculative insights.


## 43. AI workflow ROI model

**Family:** Measurement


**Use when:** You are adopting AI internally for marketing/sales productivity.


**Do not use when:** Do not measure only tool subscription cost or time saved.


**Why it works:** AI can model time, quality, error, governance, and revenue effects.


**Mindset:** Be an operator.


**Primary metrics:** Cycle time, quality score, error rate, cost saved, revenue lift.


**Failure modes:** Ignoring review time and risk costs.


**First safety rule:** Compare end-to-end workflow, not isolated prompt task.


## 44. Buyer concierge chatbot

**Family:** Agentic


**Use when:** Your site has complex buyer questions and docs/pricing/security content.


**Do not use when:** Do not deploy it when answers can be wrong or compliance-sensitive without guardrails.


**Why it works:** A chatbot can reduce search friction and route buyers to proof.


**Mindset:** Be an informed guide with visible limits.


**Primary metrics:** Qualified conversations, escalation rate, answer accuracy, demo/trial assists.


**Failure modes:** Hallucination, privacy leaks, bad handoffs.


**First safety rule:** Use retrieval from approved docs, human handoff, logs, and refusal rules.


## 45. Website conversion agent

**Family:** Agentic


**Use when:** You want AI to recommend next steps based on visitor context.


**Do not use when:** Do not create manipulative adaptive dark patterns.


**Why it works:** Agents can match content/proof to intent and reduce navigation effort.


**Mindset:** Be a librarian plus concierge.


**Primary metrics:** Content engagement, conversion, user satisfaction, no. of assisted paths.


**Failure modes:** Creepy personalization and uncontrolled decisions.


**First safety rule:** Keep recommendations explainable and reversible.


## 46. Campaign QA agent

**Family:** Agentic


**Use when:** You launch many AI-generated campaigns/assets and need safety checks.


**Do not use when:** Do not rely on it as sole legal/brand review.


**Why it works:** AI can check claims, broken links, UTMs, tone, policy, and brand consistency.


**Mindset:** Be a compliance inspector.


**Primary metrics:** Error caught before launch, approval speed, rework reduction.


**Failure modes:** False negatives on legal issues.


**First safety rule:** Use as first-pass QA, not final authority.


## 47. Knowledge base/RAG for marketing

**Family:** Agentic/Ops


**Use when:** Teams repeatedly ask for approved claims, proof, personas, and docs.


**Do not use when:** Do not include unapproved or stale data.


**Why it works:** RAG reduces hallucination by grounding AI in trusted sources.


**Mindset:** Be a source librarian.


**Primary metrics:** Answer accuracy, source citation rate, team adoption.


**Failure modes:** Garbage in/garbage out, stale proof.


**First safety rule:** Version approved sources and expire old claims.


## 48. Lead-routing and qualification assistant

**Family:** Agentic


**Use when:** Inbound volume is high and qualification logic is clear.


**Do not use when:** Do not automate rejection for high-stakes or ambiguous prospects without review.


**Why it works:** AI can summarize fit, urgency, and next best action from forms/chats.


**Mindset:** Be a triage operator.


**Primary metrics:** Speed-to-lead, meeting quality, qualification accuracy.


**Failure modes:** Bias, missed strategic accounts.


**First safety rule:** Use rules + human review for edge cases.


## 49. AI shopping / agent feed preparation

**Family:** Agentic Commerce


**Use when:** Your products may be discovered by AI assistants and shopping/research agents.


**Do not use when:** Do not optimize only human landing pages.


**Why it works:** Machine agents need structured, current product/pricing/policy/proof data.


**Mindset:** Be machine-readable and trustworthy.


**Primary metrics:** AI referrals, citation quality, product-data accuracy, checkout completion.


**Failure modes:** Stale feeds, hallucinated descriptions, missing constraints.


**First safety rule:** Publish clear feeds, schema, policies, comparisons, and support docs.


## 50. Claims substantiation checker

**Family:** Governance


**Use when:** You generate many ads/pages and need to prevent unsupported claims.


**Do not use when:** Do not publish 'best', 'guaranteed', or quantified claims without proof.


**Why it works:** AI can flag risky claims and ask for evidence.


**Mindset:** Be a prosecutor against your own hype.


**Primary metrics:** Claim pass rate, legal review findings, correction count.


**Failure modes:** Overclaiming AI outcomes, unverifiable superiority.


**First safety rule:** Keep a claim ledger with source/evidence/date/owner.


## 51. AI disclosure decision tree

**Family:** Governance


**Use when:** You use AI in customer-facing content or product workflows.


**Do not use when:** Do not assume disclosure always solves trust or legal issues.


**Why it works:** AI can help classify materiality, user expectation, and regulatory risk.


**Mindset:** Be transparent where it matters; do not make empty disclosure theatre.


**Primary metrics:** Trust, complaints, compliance findings, conversion effect.


**Failure modes:** Disclosure that erodes trust without explaining value/control.


**First safety rule:** Disclose when AI involvement affects user expectations, choices, or rights.


## 52. Bias and vulnerability review

**Family:** Governance


**Use when:** Personalization, targeting, or AI advice could exploit or exclude vulnerable users.


**Do not use when:** Do not use sensitive traits or anxiety/fear-based exploitation.


**Why it works:** AI can surface potential harms, but humans own decisions.


**Mindset:** Be a guardian of autonomy.


**Primary metrics:** Complaints, exclusion analysis, opt-outs, fairness checks.


**Failure modes:** Predatory personalization and discrimination.


**First safety rule:** Review high-risk audiences, claims, and targeting criteria.


## 53. Prompt-injection/security review

**Family:** Governance


**Use when:** You deploy public chatbots, RAG agents, or tool-using agents.


**Do not use when:** Do not give agents tools they do not need.


**Why it works:** AI systems can be manipulated through user inputs, retrieved content, or tools.


**Mindset:** Be a security architect.


**Primary metrics:** Attack test pass rate, leakage rate, tool misuse incidents.


**Failure modes:** Excessive agency, data leaks, insecure outputs.


**First safety rule:** Apply OWASP LLM controls: least privilege, input isolation, output validation.


## 54. Copyright/IP/source review

**Family:** Governance


**Use when:** You generate images, video, copy, or training data-derived outputs.


**Do not use when:** Do not use style imitation, likeness, or copyrighted assets recklessly.


**Why it works:** AI can identify potential IP and publicity-rights concerns as first pass.


**Mindset:** Be rights-conscious.


**Primary metrics:** Review flags, takedowns, asset approval time.


**Failure modes:** Accidental copying, unauthorized likeness, brand damage.


**First safety rule:** Use licensed tools/assets and document provenance.

---

## File: `22_ai_method_matrix_csv.md`

# Ai Method Matrix

Converted from `22_ai_method_matrix.csv` so the pack remains Markdown-only.

| method | family | when_to_use | when_not_to_use | why_it_works | mindset | metrics | failure_modes | first_safety_rule |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Review mining with LLMs | Research | You have many reviews, comments, forum posts, support tickets, or competitor reviews. | Do not use it as final truth. Use it to find patterns to verify with humans. | LLMs cluster language, objections, jobs, and emotional triggers faster than manual scanning. | Be a forensic analyst. Extract buyer language; do not invent it. | Quote frequency, themes found, verified interviews, conversion lift from using real language. | Hallucinated quotes, overfitting to loud users, missing silent churn reasons. | Ask the model to quote exact source lines and separate evidence from inference. |
| Interview guide generation | Research | You need to run JTBD or buyer interviews and want sharper questions quickly. | Do not let AI interview people without supervision for sensitive/high-value research. | AI can create question ladders, probes, and bias checks from your hypotheses. | Be a disciplined investigator, not a pitchman. | Interview completion, quality of surprising insights, number of falsified assumptions. | Leading questions that confirm your desired answer. | Ask AI to rewrite questions to avoid selling, leading, or assuming. |
| Transcript coding and theme extraction | Research | You have call/interview transcripts and need patterns across objections, triggers, and decision criteria. | Do not replace human review when stakes are high or sample is small. | AI accelerates qualitative coding and can compare segments. | Be a judge weighing evidence. | Intercoder agreement, theme stability, exact quotes, actionability. | Overgeneralizing from a few charismatic quotes. | Force line citations and keep a human-coded sample. |
| Synthetic customer simulation | Research | You need hypotheses before spending money on interviews or ads. | Do not treat synthetic customers as evidence of real demand. | LLMs can simulate plausible perspectives and expose blind spots, but they reflect training data and prompt framing. | Be a war-gamer. Use it to prepare, not decide. | Number of hypotheses generated, later validation rate, discarded bad assumptions. | Believing fake certainty, persona stereotypes. | Label all synthetic output as speculation until validated. |
| Competitor review triangulation | Research | You need to find gaps in competitors’ products, pricing, onboarding, docs, and support. | Do not scrape or use private/prohibited data. | Combines review mining, feature comparison, and objection mapping. | Be an intelligence analyst. | Repeated competitor complaints, angle quality, proof gaps identified. | Cherry-picking weakness while ignoring competitor strengths. | Use multiple sources: G2/Capterra/forums/docs/pricing pages. |
| Support-ticket opportunity mining | Research | You have tickets/chats and need upgrade triggers, churn risk, onboarding friction, and feature demand. | Do not use private customer data in public AI tools without approval. | Tickets reveal real friction in buyers' own language. | Be a product-growth diagnostician. | Ticket categories, support burden reduced, upgrade opportunities, churn themes. | Privacy breach, sampling only angry users. | De-identify data and cluster by job/problem, not only feature request. |
| Intent query clustering | Research | You have search-console, keyword, paid search, or site-search data. | Do not chase keywords unrelated to your ICP or ability to serve. | AI can cluster by intent stage and map content/ad offers. | Be a traffic controller. | High-intent clusters, CAC by cluster, demo/trial rate by intent. | Keyword volume addiction and low-quality leads. | Map every cluster to buyer state and proof asset. |
| Market map synthesis | Research | You need to understand category structure, alternatives, substitutes, and buyer comparison logic. | Do not use unverified AI market maps in investor/customer materials. | AI can synthesize category players, alternatives, and language fast. | Be a cartographer. | Completeness, missing alternatives found, stakeholder validation. | False competitors, stale data. | Ground with public sources and buyer interviews. |
| ICP pressure test | Strategy | You have an ICP hypothesis and need to sharpen who should and should not buy. | Do not broaden ICP because AI found many possible segments. | AI can stress-test urgency, budget, trigger events, and proof needs. | Be ruthless. Narrow until the sales motion becomes obvious. | Segment win rate, sales cycle, activation, retention, support load. | Attractive but unprofitable segments. | Force disqualification criteria. |
| JTBD map generation | Strategy | You need to connect product features to progress customers want to make. | Do not stop at generic pains like 'save time'. | AI can draft job maps and forces/struggles from research inputs. | Be a systems mapper. | Clarity of job, interview confirmation, message relevance. | Invented jobs detached from real buying moments. | Use only real quotes as inputs when possible. |
| Positioning hypothesis lab | Strategy | You need several positioning options before choosing a market frame. | Do not let AI choose positioning by prettiness. | AI can generate category, enemy, alternative, and differentiated-value frames. | Be a strategist choosing a battlefield. | Message comprehension, recall, qualified conversion, sales feedback. | Me-too claims, impossible-to-prove differentiation. | Tie every claim to evidence and buyer alternative. |
| Proof architecture design | Strategy | You know claims but not what proof must surround them. | Do not rely on testimonials when buyers need technical/security proof. | AI can map claims to evidence types: demo, docs, reviews, ROI, security, case study. | Be an evidence architect. | Proof completeness, drop-off reduction, sales objections reduced. | Claim-proof mismatch. | For every claim ask: what would a skeptical buyer inspect next? |
| Objection-to-asset matrix | Strategy | Sales hears recurring objections. | Do not answer every objection with more copy; sometimes fix product/pricing. | AI converts objections into needed assets and tests. | Be a friction eliminator. | Objection frequency, win rate, sales-cycle reduction. | Defending weaknesses instead of resolving them. | Classify objections: misunderstanding, risk, missing capability, pricing, trust. |
| Category narrative builder | Strategy | You need to explain why the old way is breaking and why the category matters now. | Do not manufacture false urgency or fear. | AI can generate narrative arcs and evidence needs. | Be a teacher of change. | Audience comprehension, content engagement, inbound quality. | Dramatic but unsupported narratives. | Back the narrative with sources and customer reality. |
| Pricing-page critique | Strategy | You need to reduce pricing confusion and increase confidence. | Do not hide fees or manipulate plans. | AI can inspect clarity, plan boundaries, risk reversal, and upgrade logic. | Be a risk reducer. | Pricing-page conversion, sales questions, refund/churn, plan-fit accuracy. | Dark patterns, bait-and-switch, hidden limits. | Require plain-language total cost and who each plan is for. |
| AI feature value framing | Strategy | You sell AI features and need to avoid AI-washing. | Do not lead with 'AI-powered' if the buyer cares about outcome and risk. | AI can translate technical capability into buyer jobs, limitations, and proof. | Be concrete: what task changes, how much, and what risk remains? | AI feature adoption, time-to-value, retention, support tickets. | Overclaiming autonomous capability. | State limitation and human-control boundaries. |
| Angle matrix generation | Creative | You need many honest ways to frame the same offer for testing. | Do not create manipulative fear/shame angles. | AI can combine jobs, emotions, proof types, and channels into testable angles. | Be a portfolio manager of hypotheses. | CTR, CVR, lead quality, downstream activation. | Volume without insight; repetitive AI slop. | Generate angles from real research; kill weak ones fast. |
| Hook stress test | Creative | You have ad hooks and need to identify vague, hypey, or unbelievable claims. | Do not optimize hooks only for clicks. | AI can evaluate specificity, inspectability, skepticism, and buyer fit. | Be skeptical like your buyer. | Hook believability, qualified CTR, bounce rate, comments. | Clickbait that poisons trust. | Ask: what proof does this hook force us to show? |
| Ad variant factory | Creative | You need structured ad variants across pain, proof, demo, comparison, and risk-reversal angles. | Do not flood channels without a test design. | AI lowers production cost; testing determines truth. | Be an experimenter, not a spammer. | Incremental conversions, CAC, creative fatigue, lead quality. | Confounded tests and brand dilution. | Limit variables: one hypothesis per variant group. |
| Landing page proof rewrite | Creative | You have traffic but skeptical visitors are not converting. | Do not solve a broken product or wrong audience with copy. | AI can reorganize pages by buyer questions, proof, objections, and CTA sequence. | Be a guide through uncertainty. | Scroll depth, demo/trial clicks, conversion rate, sales-fit. | Overlong pages with no real proof. | Map each section to a buyer question. |
| Demo script generation | Creative | You need product demos that show the buyer's job, not feature inventory. | Do not create fake demos that hide constraints. | AI can structure demos around before/after workflow and decision criteria. | Be a show-don't-tell operator. | Demo completion, sales conversion, activation from demo viewers. | Demo theatre; no operational reality. | Use real workflows and name limitations. |
| Video storyboard / UGC concepting | Creative | You need creative concepts for short-form or explainer videos. | Do not fake customers or endorsements. | AI can draft scenes, cuts, hooks, and proof beats. | Be a director of evidence, not spectacle. | View-through, qualified clicks, watch time, trial starts. | Synthetic authenticity that feels fake. | Anchor every scene in a real job or proof point. |
| Founder-led content drafting | Creative | You need expertise-rich content that earns trust. | Do not let AI manufacture personal experience. | AI can outline, challenge, and polish; founder must supply judgment and stories. | Be the expert; AI is the editor. | Engagement by ICP, replies, referrals, branded search. | Generic thought leadership. | Require specific examples, tradeoffs, failures, and lessons. |
| GEO / AI-answer content brief | Creative | You need content that answer engines can cite and summarize accurately. | Do not stuff keywords or write for bots only. | AI can structure clear definitions, comparisons, evidence, FAQs, and citations. | Be a librarian building reliable evidence objects. | AI search citations, referral quality, branded queries, citation share. | Thin AI spam, hallucinated claims. | Use primary sources, schema, clear authorship, and dated facts. |
| Case study builder | Creative | You need proof assets from customer wins. | Do not invent metrics or cherry-pick nonrepresentative success. | AI can structure problem, context, intervention, result, tradeoffs, and implementation details. | Be a witness preparing evidence. | Case study influenced pipeline, sales-cycle reduction, objection reduction. | Vague success stories with no baseline. | Include baseline, constraints, numbers, and buyer quotes. |
| Comparison page generator | Creative | Buyers compare you with alternatives. | Do not attack competitors unfairly or hide where they are better. | AI can organize fair comparison tables, use cases, migration considerations, and fit/no-fit guidance. | Be a trusted advisor. | Comparison-page conversion, sales assist, AI-search visibility. | Biased comparison that destroys trust. | Include 'choose competitor when...' sections. |
| Documentation-to-marketing repurposing | Creative | Technical docs contain buyer proof. | Do not turn precise docs into vague slogans. | AI can turn docs into buyer explainers, diagrams, FAQs, and demo scripts. | Be a translator, not a simplifier of truth. | Docs-assisted conversion, developer activation, reduced support questions. | Loss of technical accuracy. | Have SMEs review every technical claim. |
| Segment-specific page generation | Personalization | You have distinct ICPs with different jobs and proof needs. | Do not create thousands of shallow pages. | AI helps adapt messaging and proof to segment context. | Be relevant without becoming creepy. | Segment CVR, time on page, pipeline quality. | Thin duplicate content, privacy concerns. | Use segment-level context, not invasive personal data. |
| Account-based message drafting | Personalization | You have target accounts and public context. | Do not imply knowledge from private surveillance. | AI can tailor outreach around public triggers, tech stack, initiatives, and likely jobs. | Be useful, specific, and respectful. | Reply rate, meeting quality, unsubscribe/spam rate. | Creepy personalization, hallucinated company facts. | Only use verifiable public facts and cite internal sources. |
| Lifecycle email sequence design | Lifecycle | Users sign up but fail to activate or upgrade. | Do not bombard users who have not shown intent. | AI can map emails to activation events, objections, and milestones. | Be a coach guiding progress. | Activation, feature adoption, trial-to-paid, unsubscribes. | Too much email, low relevance. | Trigger from behavior and value milestones. |
| In-app nudge copy | Lifecycle | Users are in product and need help taking next useful action. | Do not interrupt deep work or use guilt/pressure. | AI can produce concise nudges by state and goal. | Be a helpful guide inside the task. | Feature adoption, completion rate, nudge dismissal rate. | Manipulative interruptions. | Use reversible, dismissible, context-aware prompts. |
| Churn-risk message generation | Lifecycle | A user shows inactivity or downgrade risk. | Do not use fear or make cancellation hard. | AI can tailor help, education, and save offers to likely reason. | Be a problem solver, not a hostage taker. | Retention lift, save rate, satisfaction after save. | Annoying users who want to leave. | Make cancellation clear; offer help, data export, or downgrade. |
| Customer education content lab | Lifecycle | Buyers need to become successful users after purchase. | Do not treat education as upsell spam. | AI can create tutorials, checklists, examples, and role-specific enablement. | Be the adoption architect. | Time-to-value, completion, support tickets, expansion. | Generic content no one uses. | Tie content to activation milestones and jobs. |
| Sales-call brief generator | Sales | A rep needs to understand account context and likely objections quickly. | Do not use unverified AI guesses as facts. | AI summarizes public data, CRM notes, prior calls, and likely agenda. | Be prepared, not performative. | Call quality, discovery depth, next-step conversion. | Hallucinated facts and overpersonalization. | Separate verified facts from hypotheses. |
| Objection handler / battlecard | Sales | Reps need concise answers to competitor, pricing, security, and implementation concerns. | Do not script manipulative pressure lines. | AI can generate answer paths and proof assets. | Be a calm expert lowering risk. | Win rate in common objection scenarios, rep adoption. | Robotic scripts and unsupported claims. | Link every answer to proof. |
| Proposal / RFP assistant | Sales | You need to respond consistently and fast to structured buyer requirements. | Do not auto-submit unreviewed answers, especially legal/security claims. | AI drafts from approved knowledge base and past answers. | Be exact and compliant. | Response time, error rate, win rate, legal rework. | Fabricated commitments. | Use retrieval from approved source only and SME review. |
| Post-demo follow-up personalization | Sales | A buyer attended a demo and needs a useful next step. | Do not send generic recap spam. | AI can map demo topics to proof links, next actions, and stakeholder-specific notes. | Be a project manager for buyer confidence. | Reply rate, stakeholder forwards, next meeting conversion. | Overlong recaps and false promises. | Include only what was discussed plus relevant proof. |
| Customer success expansion map | Sales/CS | You want ethical expansion based on proven usage and unmet jobs. | Do not upsell before value is achieved. | AI can identify account patterns, missing workflows, and expansion hypotheses. | Be a steward of customer value. | Expansion rate, NRR, satisfaction, usage depth. | Pushing features instead of outcomes. | Tie expansion to customer goals and demonstrated value. |
| Experiment design assistant | Measurement | You need to test AI-generated creatives, pages, emails, or workflows. | Do not let AI pick winners from underpowered data. | AI can draft hypotheses, sample needs, guardrails, and interpretation checks. | Be a scientist of buyer behavior. | Test validity, lift, confidence/credible intervals, downstream quality. | Peeking, multiple-comparison false positives. | Pre-register hypothesis and success metrics. |
| Attribution critique agent | Measurement | Your dashboard says a campaign works but you are unsure if it is incremental. | Do not equate last-click ROAS with causal lift. | AI can interrogate measurement assumptions and suggest holdouts/lift tests. | Be suspicious of convenient numbers. | Incrementality evidence, channel mix quality, CAC payback. | Dashboard worship. | Ask: what would have happened without this? |
| Creative fatigue monitor | Measurement | High-performing ads decline over time. | Do not react to noise with constant random changes. | AI can detect pattern shifts and suggest new variants by winning theme. | Be a portfolio manager. | Frequency, CTR decay, CPA drift, qualitative comments. | Over-rotating before statistical signal. | Refresh within proven angles before abandoning them. |
| Insight extraction from experiments | Measurement | You have results but need learning, not just winner/loser. | Do not overread tiny differences. | AI can summarize patterns by segment, channel, and creative angle. | Be a learning accountant. | Reusable insights, future test quality, documented decisions. | Post-hoc storytelling. | Separate confirmed, suggestive, and speculative insights. |
| AI workflow ROI model | Measurement | You are adopting AI internally for marketing/sales productivity. | Do not measure only tool subscription cost or time saved. | AI can model time, quality, error, governance, and revenue effects. | Be an operator. | Cycle time, quality score, error rate, cost saved, revenue lift. | Ignoring review time and risk costs. | Compare end-to-end workflow, not isolated prompt task. |
| Buyer concierge chatbot | Agentic | Your site has complex buyer questions and docs/pricing/security content. | Do not deploy it when answers can be wrong or compliance-sensitive without guardrails. | A chatbot can reduce search friction and route buyers to proof. | Be an informed guide with visible limits. | Qualified conversations, escalation rate, answer accuracy, demo/trial assists. | Hallucination, privacy leaks, bad handoffs. | Use retrieval from approved docs, human handoff, logs, and refusal rules. |
| Website conversion agent | Agentic | You want AI to recommend next steps based on visitor context. | Do not create manipulative adaptive dark patterns. | Agents can match content/proof to intent and reduce navigation effort. | Be a librarian plus concierge. | Content engagement, conversion, user satisfaction, no. of assisted paths. | Creepy personalization and uncontrolled decisions. | Keep recommendations explainable and reversible. |
| Campaign QA agent | Agentic | You launch many AI-generated campaigns/assets and need safety checks. | Do not rely on it as sole legal/brand review. | AI can check claims, broken links, UTMs, tone, policy, and brand consistency. | Be a compliance inspector. | Error caught before launch, approval speed, rework reduction. | False negatives on legal issues. | Use as first-pass QA, not final authority. |
| Knowledge base/RAG for marketing | Agentic/Ops | Teams repeatedly ask for approved claims, proof, personas, and docs. | Do not include unapproved or stale data. | RAG reduces hallucination by grounding AI in trusted sources. | Be a source librarian. | Answer accuracy, source citation rate, team adoption. | Garbage in/garbage out, stale proof. | Version approved sources and expire old claims. |
| Lead-routing and qualification assistant | Agentic | Inbound volume is high and qualification logic is clear. | Do not automate rejection for high-stakes or ambiguous prospects without review. | AI can summarize fit, urgency, and next best action from forms/chats. | Be a triage operator. | Speed-to-lead, meeting quality, qualification accuracy. | Bias, missed strategic accounts. | Use rules + human review for edge cases. |
| AI shopping / agent feed preparation | Agentic Commerce | Your products may be discovered by AI assistants and shopping/research agents. | Do not optimize only human landing pages. | Machine agents need structured, current product/pricing/policy/proof data. | Be machine-readable and trustworthy. | AI referrals, citation quality, product-data accuracy, checkout completion. | Stale feeds, hallucinated descriptions, missing constraints. | Publish clear feeds, schema, policies, comparisons, and support docs. |
| Claims substantiation checker | Governance | You generate many ads/pages and need to prevent unsupported claims. | Do not publish 'best', 'guaranteed', or quantified claims without proof. | AI can flag risky claims and ask for evidence. | Be a prosecutor against your own hype. | Claim pass rate, legal review findings, correction count. | Overclaiming AI outcomes, unverifiable superiority. | Keep a claim ledger with source/evidence/date/owner. |
| AI disclosure decision tree | Governance | You use AI in customer-facing content or product workflows. | Do not assume disclosure always solves trust or legal issues. | AI can help classify materiality, user expectation, and regulatory risk. | Be transparent where it matters; do not make empty disclosure theatre. | Trust, complaints, compliance findings, conversion effect. | Disclosure that erodes trust without explaining value/control. | Disclose when AI involvement affects user expectations, choices, or rights. |
| Bias and vulnerability review | Governance | Personalization, targeting, or AI advice could exploit or exclude vulnerable users. | Do not use sensitive traits or anxiety/fear-based exploitation. | AI can surface potential harms, but humans own decisions. | Be a guardian of autonomy. | Complaints, exclusion analysis, opt-outs, fairness checks. | Predatory personalization and discrimination. | Review high-risk audiences, claims, and targeting criteria. |
| Prompt-injection/security review | Governance | You deploy public chatbots, RAG agents, or tool-using agents. | Do not give agents tools they do not need. | AI systems can be manipulated through user inputs, retrieved content, or tools. | Be a security architect. | Attack test pass rate, leakage rate, tool misuse incidents. | Excessive agency, data leaks, insecure outputs. | Apply OWASP LLM controls: least privilege, input isolation, output validation. |
| Copyright/IP/source review | Governance | You generate images, video, copy, or training data-derived outputs. | Do not use style imitation, likeness, or copyrighted assets recklessly. | AI can identify potential IP and publicity-rights concerns as first pass. | Be rights-conscious. | Review flags, takedowns, asset approval time. | Accidental copying, unauthorized likeness, brand damage. | Use licensed tools/assets and document provenance. |

---

## File: `23_generative_ai_marketing_buying_system.md`

# Generative AI and the Buying System

## The core shift

Generative AI does not change the fundamental buyer equation:

> Purchase likelihood = problem intensity × perceived value × trust × ease × timing − perceived risk.

It changes how fast you can research, create, personalize, test, route, and support that equation.

The old digital marketing stack was mostly:
- search ads,
- social ads,
- landing pages,
- email,
- analytics,
- sales calls,
- customer success,
- reviews.

The GenAI marketing stack adds:
- AI-assisted customer research,
- synthetic hypothesis generation,
- high-velocity creative production,
- AI-assisted personalization,
- AI search and answer-engine visibility,
- conversational buyer assistance,
- agentic shopping/research flows,
- internal sales/customer-success copilots,
- automated QA and governance,
- richer proof architecture.

The danger is obvious: when output cost collapses, mediocre operators flood the market with generic AI slop. The advantage goes to the person who uses AI to become more specific, more evidence-based, more useful, and more trustworthy.

## The correct mental model

Do not think:

> "AI will persuade people for me."

Think:

> "AI will help me find the buyer's real context, build better proof, create more hypotheses, test faster, and remove friction from the inspection path."

For skeptical buyers, the ad still does not need to be believed. The ad needs to earn inspection. GenAI can improve that inspection path if it helps you create better demos, documentation, comparison pages, FAQs, calculators, case studies, onboarding flows, and proof assets.

## What GenAI changes in the buyer journey

### 1. Problem recognition

GenAI helps you find the language buyers use before they know what category they need.

Useful methods:
- review mining,
- support-ticket mining,
- Reddit/forum synthesis,
- search-query clustering,
- sales-call theme extraction,
- interview guide generation.

Mindset:

> Find the buyer's words before writing your words.

Do not use AI to invent pain. Use it to extract and organize pain from real evidence.

### 2. Category belief

A buyer may feel pain but not believe software is worth adopting. AI helps create educational assets that show:
- why the old workflow is costly,
- what changed in the environment,
- what alternatives exist,
- how the product category solves the job,
- how adoption risk can be reduced.

Useful methods:
- category narrative builder,
- educational content briefs,
- ROI model drafts,
- comparison explainers,
- "manual workflow vs automated workflow" demos.

Mindset:

> Teach the market before asking it to choose you.

### 3. Vendor belief

A buyer now believes the category might help. They need to decide whether your product deserves inspection.

Useful methods:
- positioning hypothesis lab,
- competitor review triangulation,
- proof architecture design,
- case study builder,
- comparison page generator,
- product demo scripting.

Mindset:

> Specificity beats hype. Demonstration beats adjectives.

### 4. Risk reduction

Software buyers rarely fear the price alone. They fear:
- switching effort,
- implementation failure,
- security/privacy risk,
- embarrassment,
- hidden costs,
- vendor lock-in,
- no internal adoption,
- wrong decision in front of colleagues.

GenAI helps you map risks and produce the evidence needed to reduce them.

Useful methods:
- objection-to-asset matrix,
- pricing-page critique,
- security/FAQ assistant,
- onboarding risk map,
- proposal/RFP assistant,
- claims substantiation checker.

Mindset:

> Every unhandled objection is a leak in the buying system.

### 5. Action trigger

A trigger is not only an ad. It can be:
- an AI search citation,
- a founder post,
- a review snippet,
- a comparison page,
- a retargeting ad,
- a direct message,
- a demo video,
- a free tool,
- a trial prompt,
- an in-app nudge,
- a buyer-agent recommendation.

GenAI helps create and personalize triggers, but the trigger must match the buyer state.

Mindset:

> The right next step is better than the loudest CTA.

### 6. Product experience

For software, product experience often creates the strongest belief. AI can improve:
- guided onboarding,
- in-product help,
- personalized setup checklists,
- example data,
- support triage,
